video_title: Interactions with the World’s Most Personable Turtle! 😳 #turtle #tortoise #gardenstatetortoise
text_content: People told me turtles are just solitary and don’t interact much. However, my YBS is so active. She signals when she wants out of her tank to go outside for a walk, loves to give nose kisses, and will actively walk into the hands of neighbors when out on a walk if she likes them. She also does yoga on her underwater platform for hours. She’s like a puppy. I love her so much.

text_content: We 'won' one of these at a carnival around 1999.  I didn't follow the recommended feeding instructions or store bought food and thought the turtle might enjoy some fresh feeder goldfish.  Yes, it did, but as I put something like 25 feeder goldfish into its tank thinking it would eat one when it was hungry, the turtle proceeded to eat EVERY single goldfish within like 30 minutes--it kept trying to eat even though it wasn't hungry anymore leaving 1/2 to 3/4 of the fish (until later).  We tried to incorporate other fish and even a crab into the tank (which the fish store owner insisted would be fine) and it ate crabby and the fish.  We went back to the recommended store bought food and the thing grew quite large from the original carnival size.  We lived near Cal State University, Northridge, so on New Year's Day 2000, we scooped the turtle out of its tank and took it to a pond they had (maybe still do) on campus which was filled with fish and other wildlife (no, not meant to be a pond filled with native aquatic creatures, but probably had fish caught around the Valley like at the Sepulveda Dam and Hansen Dam).  Never did go back to check on the turtle or the pond after that...

text_content: My first turtle was one that showed up on our doorstep.  It was a desert tortoise.  It hung around for the longest time.  We fed it greens and some other veggies.  Any bugs it ate were obtained from the backyard.  We had sliding glass doors that opened up to the backyard patio.  The turtle figured out how to open the doors and come inside.  We did not cage it or anything.  It was free to go anytime it wanted.

text_content:
I "rescued" my RES named Dip from a severely neglected pond back when i was 12 years old and she was the size of a silver dollar (I totally didnt know it was bad to take animals out of the wild at the time, but i did know she was invasive and was probably going to die if left in that disgrace of a pond). She's been my roommate ever since and is creeping up to 18 years old soon!

Easily one of my most sociable pets, and demands head/shell scritches when i walk past her. Dip will cause as much noise as possible with her shell and claws on the decor and side of the tank until I pet her, only after sufficient shell scritches and head rubs will she calm down.

They sure are a lot of maintenance compared to other reptiles (so.many.water changes.), but she has definitely been worth it

text_content: There are ways to cut down on the cost of filtration. I use a hang on back built for a 50 gallon on a 40 gallon with a musk turtle. All the research I did suggests that because all the most common aquatic turtles (even the musks) are carnivores and make such a mess that I’d need something capable of handling twice the that. So a filter for an 80. Instead I just added stuff. It has a pre-filter sponge on the intake, and then I stuffed the reservoir with bio media rings and filter sponges. I also put a bunch of floating water plants in(frog bit and water spangle) along with Java moss. The filter, sponges, filter media, and plants cost me like $60 total. I clean the sponges once every week and a half to 2 weeks. I check the parameters then too and the nitrates are always nonexistent so I have to add fertilizer for the plants. There’s also a colony of guppies in there too along with one female bristle-nose pleco. Just so you understand what this filter and plants are tackling.

text_content:
Back when I was young, I found a turtle in a coulee. Took him, put him in a bucket with a rock. I fed him bugs I found in the yard or around the house. Maybe some lettuce or cabbage. Even threw in a small lizard or two. Didnt know they actually ate lizards. worms were his favorite. But he usually hated beetles because everytime he ate one, he'd spit it out. 

Id clean out his bucket and his shell when they became too mossy. Just used regular hose water. We kept him outside all year long, even when it was cold. My parents didnt want a big old bucket of stinky water in the house.

I was scared when I walked outside to see the top of the bucket water completely frozen over. Thought my turtle had froze to death. But once the ice thawed, he was alive and well every time. That must have something to do with turtles being cold blooded.

But one day it rained and the bucket overflowed with water and thats when the turtle got out and we never saw him again. We think he made it back to the coulee.

Thats how I took cared for my turtles when I was a kid. But now that Im older and my nieces and nephews want a pet turtle, Im looking for more modern ways of taking care of turles. Even though im sure the traditional methods works, I just dont want their bucket to overflow with water and the turtle manages to get out.

text_content: I’ve been herping for many years and the Bog (or Muhlenberg’s) Turtle is the ONLY species native to the Tri-State region (NY, NJ, CT) that I’ve never seen in their native habitat. I have only seen adults and hatchlings in one place; the Stony Brook Lab & Fish Hatchery on Long Island, NY. They have several pair in a breeding program because they are a critically endangered species on CITES. Their small size made them ideal pets and during the early 1900s they were over collected for the pet trade. This in combination with habitat destruction led to their near extinction in the wild.

text_content: When I was a kid, my buddy lived on a farm with some pretty big ponds on the property. The biggest pond had swans and all of a sudden they started losing feet. Turns out there was a 4ft wide, 5ft long alligator snapper in the pond. We used to swim and fish in that pond all the time. When my buddy's mom finally got a picture of the snapper up on the bank, I got chills knowing I was swimming with that thing. Noone ever swam in that pond again lol. Nope, nope, and nope. I've moved smaller ones out of the road here and there and when they snap it sounds like a trap snapping shut. Your finger would be like a toothpick. Also be careful because these sobs have long necks. Just when you think you're out of bite range, you're not. They're amazing animals that have survived forever. Looking at one up close is like looking back in time, like a living dinosaur.

video_description: Discover the top 5 aquatic turtles perfect for keeping as pets in this engaging countdown by Top Ten Temptations! From the ...
text_content: As an owner of a Yellow Bellied Slider and an Eastern Painted turtle. If I had to pick one, I would go with the painted turtle.  The Eastern and Southern Painteds grow much slower and stay small. 4-6in males, 6-8 inches for females. Mine lives happily in a 40-gallon tank. You get the joy of an aquatic turtle, same exact care requirements as sliders, but in a smaller package, and its colors are more beautiful. Red Eared Sliders get huge and require a pond, not recommended. My Yellow Belly is in a 75 gallon by herself and I only keep her because she was my first baby and I'm emotionally attached.

text_content:
Little turtles tend to be so much more work then bigger one. My big tank barley gets dirty but my little tank is a weekly clean. I got two small turtles from a friend who wasn’t expecting them to be so messy and one of them ended up getting an eye infection and potentially a respiratory infection that’s I’ve been dealing with for a month now shots every three days and weekly vitamin A. He seems like he is getting better some days his eyes are open some days they still look infected. I feel so bad but I’m doing everything possible to help him. He can’t see to eat so I have to feed him with a syringe daily. I also have 4 kids under 8 😅😅 so my life has been hectic the last month or so. But anything to help my guy get better. 😢

UPDATE: My baby turtle is no longer sick and is thriving tremendously 💕

I found a lovely one close to me that rescued and permanently homed any non native terrapin or water turtle and was happy to take them now they live with 104 other terrapins has lots of room to walk and has a massive pool that’s always at 24c I was often worried that my terrapin wasn’t getting what they needed but I was told my terrapin was strong, healthy and super engaged with their environment!! text_content:
Dude, bless you for this. It is so heartbreaking how many turtles end up neglected, dying young, or being heartlessly dumped in the wild because people don't know what they've signed up for. Anyone who even THINKS about getting a turtle for their kid in my presence is going to get a speech. These babies produce so much waste and live for SO long, and despite ehat appears to be popular belief, it's not cool to put them in a room by themselves and leave them alone all the time. It's just not something most people are prepared to deal with. That in itself sucks because they need good homes. ='( I won't get into how people treat animals in tanks/cages.

More people need to upload videos like this one. I get really upset when I see people encouraging strangers on the internet to adopt turtles without getting into the details, especially the lifelong commitment aspect.

On behalf of myself, my ASN Xeno, and all turtles, thank you so much!!!

text_content:
My husband and I have spent 2 spring/summer seasons rescuing turtles off the roads in our area. We have saved around 30 turtles of differing species and have even had to help some of them heal from injuries and malnutrition before releasing them back into the wild near where we found them, though we made sure they were a good distance from the road when releasing them. 

I am proud to say we have helped these amazing creatures and hope there are others doing similar things in their areas as well. Just stopping to help a turtle get across the road safely is a good thing to do to help lessen the number of injured or killed turtles. All it takes is a few minutes of your time and a small amount of effort on our parts. 

Thank you for another great video Clint. Can’t wait for the next one. ☺️💕

Box turtles are lovely.

Small, rounded, slow-moving and always ploughing through green and lush vegetation.

I have never seen them in person, but they are ubiquitous in most south-eastern American herping videos. This is borderline extraterrestrial.

text_content: Many moons ago, when I was doing my undergrad research on native orchids, my capable research assistant, a yellow Labrador belonging to my research advisor, kept bringing us box turtles while we were out looking for orchids.  I say “box turtles,” but there’s every chance that it was the same hapless box turtle each time.  She’d bring us a turtle and then we’d have to try and figure out where she found him so we could return him home.  The turtle or turtles never looked as pleased about the situation as Lambchop (the Labrador) did.

text_content: Living in the (sort of) north east of the east coast, my friend and I had caught a box turtle when we were around 12 years old. Neither of us knew how to keep a turtle properly, and with this being a "box" turtle... we threw it in a box on her back deck, and checked in on it daily. I know for a fact that we didn't feed it properly, nor did we provide the space and 'amenities' that they might need. Thankfully, one day it was gone and seemed to escape off of her deck. I like to think it's still alive and well, since it's only been ~10-15 years or so.

text_content:
Clint ,
I grew up (age 2 to 18) on a 27 acre farm in Jackson, N.J. , bordered on the south side by a brook(the north branch of the Metedeconk River.
Across the road from our Victorian farmhouse was another old farm with a house dating back to the mid 18th century.
It had been farmed by the Clayton family for many generations and many generations of children had played with Box Turtles which frequented the wild strawberries growing  there.
It wasn't uncommon to find turtles with "plastrons". with initials on them carved there by these children.
In the late fifties, I was visiting the field and found a huge box turtle.
On it's plastron was carved,  "Lt. Opey Y Co. 189?
I took a piece of brown paper and copied the carving just as it was on the turtle.
 I almost brought it home, almost! It had survived for so long I did not want to endanger it in any way.
I never researched him, but Lt. OPey may have been a Spanish American or possibly a First World War Veteran.
So, I've always wondered jut how old was that Turtle(Terrapin) to have had that much carving still very readable,
when Opey left his message?
I'll never know,  but  that was the most amazing Terrapin encounter I ever had.
Cheers,
Rik Spector

text_content: I kept two Ornate Box Turtles during my childhood. Both were caught crossing roads in the eastern plains of Colorado by my Dad. He was a CPA and did county audits for a number of counties in Colorado, some of them in the plains area so he did a fair amount of driving there. I kept both outdoors in a wooden box with a baking pan as a water container for a summer then released them in the fall. We fed them a variety of things, from raw ground beef to lettuce and bananas, probably other fruitt and veggies too but I don't remember them all. What both seemed to really like to eat best though were tomato hornworms. We grew tomatoes, and when I caught one of those big green caterpillars leaving its trail of devastation in the tomato garden, I would give it to the turtle.

text_content:
Used to find many in the suburban subdivision I used to live. We found 3 or 4 babies when I was a child. Last summer I found 3 in the same month in August, all within a couple hundred yard range. One was a smaller mature mature female, then there was a juvenile female and a juvenile male. All very healthy and vibrant. Sadly, the small patch of woodland that I used to walk daily for 20 years was ripped up and destroyed the following fall. The county needed to maintenance on a lot of underground water lines that passed through the forest, so now it's all gone. I used to see great blue herons, foxes, beavers, muskrats, deer, red tailed hawks, geese, woodland mallards, box turtles, occasional common snapping turtle, cooter turtles, other basking turtles, bullfrogs, raccoons and all sorts of other things there despite the woodland being surrounded on all sides by subdivisions. All gone now.

I live somewhere more rural and remote, but haven't seen a fraction of the wildlife I used to see there. It feels like a part of me was taken when they tore all those trees down and will never return.

text_content: Daughter found an injured box turtle about a month ago.  She looks like something tried to eat it.  The left eye is missing and has a crack in her shell.  She took it somewhere and was told that the eye was infected.  I tried to bring it back to health by giving it some penicillen.  It wasn't able to open it's mouth.  After a week with medicine, it was able to eat the grounded up bugs/worms that I made mush out of.  the next week, she was eating lettuce, carrots and some fruits.  Hopefully she will be able to eat live worms and more soon.  I got her to my home and she now has a great home that PetSmart and another turtle owner helped me set up.  I am afraid that she will never be able to be returned to the wild, but I take her outside everyday so that she can feel the grass and wind. Love my new pet.

text_content: Since moving to southern VA I have saved so many that get in the road, especially after it rains. The other day I pulled over to get one out of the road and while I was getting out of my car someone ran right over it. I heard the crunching of its shell & it literally broke my heart. It wasn’t even fully in the road yet so the person was either not paying ANY attention or did it on purpose. It has bothered me for over a week now. A few days ago as I was weed eating an overgrown area of tall grass around my home I saw a yellow & brown creature deep in the grass. I jumped back thinking it was likely a snake but upon closer inspection it was a turtle!! I stopped weed eating and left it alone. I’ve since seen it a couple more times around the yard. I hope it stays around. I would never want to cage it & I wasn’t sure what to do to help it thrive in my yard. We are in the country with plenty of woods so hopefully it will do well. I know that God brought that turtle to me because of what happened the other day. God’s creation is so beautiful & amazing & most people don’t even see or acknowledge it. Every day as I drive I am in awe of God’s creation all around us!

text_content: I’ve had my EBT for 30yrs now. I was 5yrs old in ‘91 when I found her wandering through our milk parlor on our dairy farm of all places & since at age 5 I was already in love with TMNT I picked her  up bc I didn’t want her to get stepped on by a cow first of all but also bc I wanted a pet turtle lol. She’s never been a shy or scared turtle at all, in fact she’s never completely “boxed up” on me that I can ever remember. Idk for sure an age and my vet isn’t totally sure either but I did learn she was a she when we first took her to a vet when I first found her. I built a big “tortoise table” for her about 17-18yrs ago when I was at college & took her with me to my apartment and she’s loved it ever since. I have a substrate side for her on 1 end with both a heat pad & light, in the middle I transitioned to a small soil area where there’s ferns, dandelions, & lilacs that she eats right out of the ground as the grow with the help of a light & misting system and then on the far side I have a sloping transition to a basking pool. Her table is 8ftlong by 3ft wide with 6inch sides around the whole frame so she doesn’t see through, walk off and hurt or kill herself by cracking or shattering her shell. This is a really great video for anyone who has an EBT they wanna improve their husbandry skills with or are thinking of getting one & doing their due diligence beforehand which I highly recommend with ANY animal you plan on getting as a pet!

text_content: About 40 years ago I was going to high school after Thanksgiving break. Here in the Mid-Atlantic it was just below freezing and a freezing drizzle was coming down. As I started my car at about 6:45 am I saw a lump in the middle of the street. I stopped to look at this little lump. It was a sweet little box turtle, probably fully grown. It wasn't moving, it was tucked in its shell. I wasn't entirely certain it was alive at first. I picked him (no idea if this is accurate) up and took him into the house. We placed him in a milk crate in a cold area of the house. He stayed with us until the weather broke. I would set him outside in his crate during the day and bring him in at night. It would get down near freezing at night and as warm as 50sF in the day.  One day in late April, the crate was over on its side and he was gone. I hope he lived a long turtle life after that.

Last thing is buy a box turtle along with a variety of food (following your advice) to see what the turtle likes. I won't expect it to eat anything on the first day due to most likely it being stressed from being transport but I will give it a meal variety anyway.

text_content: This turtle was a companion not a pet. I once had a turtle that I found about the size of a quarter. It was after a storm. At first I thought it was dead. So I picked it up so my dog wouldn't have. I was going to bury it and on my way to a shovel it moved. I was determined to get it big enough to not worry about its survival. So I kept it in my shirt pocket everywhere I went. Made sure it got water and food. After a few weeks buster was in my opinion big enough to be on his own. Even my dog rocky didn't mind him being around. So I put him by the pond. Buster never went. He came back to the steps. He became a companion. Anywhere in the yard I went buster was trying to follow. It took years for buster to finally become a turtle. I sometimes go sit in the swing by the pond and buster would come sit beside me. A couple years later the pond all but dried up and buster moved on. Rocky passed away shortly after buster left. I can only pray buster is still out there being a turtle.

text_content: I would add info on the water requirement.    I put my 7 box turtles in my bathtub  everyday or two  during those months when they were not hibernating.  Five or six inches of water.  They loved it.  Sometimes they submerged themselves.  They never got dehydrated.  I fed them a variety of food after their bath.  I had a worm farm in my refridge that I bought from WalMart.  They sucked the worms up like spaghetti and sometimes fought over the same worm.  Okay, I'm fanatical. I gave them the freedom to go anywhere in my house.  I found them in my shoes in the closet, or shelved sideways among the books in my bookshelf.  I kept them outdoors during the summer and finally released them into a remote sanctuary with a creek.  It broke my heart but I had become disabled and had no choice.  It was worse than having a child leave me with an empty nest.

text_content: Box turtles are actually very strong swimmers, their lungs function as a float and will keep them buoyant in deeper water.  A good substrate for box turtles are similar to what the BioDude has, the Terra Firma.  My movers busted a 180 gallon aquarium and I made it into an enclosure for my Box Turtle-Hippie (lol-brother named her when he was 10) .  She has had many enclosures and my brother started her (he though she was a he back then too) in a 10 gallon with AstroTurf and plastic plants.  Lots more info out there now as to what is better for them than there was 30-ish years ago, lol.  Franklin is a cutie and his new home is great.  I had Hippie in a 50 gallon Rubbermaid but having spent most of her life in a glass enclosure she hated it and spent all of her time trying to climb out. Tomorrow will be her first day in the 180 gallon, I even made her a water feature out of the 1/3 of the tank, complete with waterfall and rocky soaking area.  I hope she likes it, lol.

text_content:
I have a really sad box turtle story from when I was a kid, as a cautionary tale. I was pretty good as far as taking care of him, I was a reptile nerd so I did research in these things called “books” that in hind sight were probably from the 70’s lol. He had a decently balanced diet of veggies, bugs and worms, and vitamin supplements. He was good natured, never bit, and often crawled toward me and other people, so he couldn’t have been that miserable lol. I had him in an aquarium with bark bedding like everyone else did back then. He had the proper lights and I was aware they liked water so I let him play in the sink almost every day. Not great husbandry, but in the late 80’s most of the info you got was from the pet store where you got them, let alone as a 10 year old kid. Anyway, my turtle loved bananas. We went on vacation, he sat down on a piece of banana we left for him- and it dried under him, and he got stuck to it on the bottom of the aquarium. He couldn’t get to his water dish and he died. An awful death to be sure. 

Use these excellent resources to do a better job than we knew to back in the day, they are excellent pets. I personally think they’re fine for beginners, mainly because their temp needs are minimal, and their food is relatively cheap and easily attainable. Gone are the days of throwing a goldfish in a bowl and having them live for 6 months, and our pets are much better off for it.

text_content: Mulch and bark isn't the best substrate for these guys. I have mine on 50% eco earth 50% peat moss. Rocks larger than his head around his water dish will help prevent him tracking dirt in.  A lot of mulch and barks are toxic and can cause damage to their eyes. It is worth noting that cat food or dog food is not good I know you said you don't feed it to him but just for anyone creeping in the comments. Great job with Franklin he looks like he has an amazing life now takes a lot to admit your mistakes and right them. Fantastic!

text_content: I had a three-toed as my first pet as well. I also made some early mistakes, mostly covered by the video, but I will let y'all know what an exotic vet told me when I had to take my buddy in for a trim. Feeding them wet cat food for protein is not recommended because of the phosphorus content (especially for the younger turtles). With that big ol shell, they need all the calcium they can get, but because of the way they metabolize minerals, the phosphorus in cat food increases their required calcium even higher to an almost unobtainable level. This can lead to shell deformities and weaker/lethargic turtles. Varied diet is key!

text_content: I have a Desert Box Turtle named Diego. She showed up to my back porch full grown with dog chew holes on her shell about 25 years ago and we've been best friends ever since. She's my childhood pet and I still have her living in my room for winter with all her lights in a 75 Gallon Tank. I move her tank to an outside enclosed porch around April because she hates refrigerated air and bring her back inside the first week of October. I never let her play outside alone unless I'm with her because I don't want to lose her. She's about the size of a McDonalds Cheeseburger. I have to admit that when I first got her I had no idea what I was doing, or if she had a family that she never returned to and I feel bad about that but now I'm commited to her because we moved over a decade ago and she came with. I have no idea how she got into the yard because the yard was completely fenced off and there were no holes under the fence. She just appeared at the back door. Now that she's getting older she's very active and happy and healthy but I can't get her to eat anything other than bananas (with bird vitamins 2x a week) and hamburger meat. She loves those but will absolutely not accept any other food. I feed her every day even if she doesn't eat but it's offered. I take her for a yearly check-up every beginning of October at the veterinarian and the doctor says she looks healthy. We're not sure how old she is but she was full grown when she joined my family. I'm Autistic and don't have any children or a wife of my own and I wonder if my childhood beloved pet is going to outlive me. What's she going to do if I die before her? I feel that releasing her into the wild (especially after decades in captivity and in a totally different location) is unthinkable after being in captivity for so long and having her needs taken care of but what's she going to do if I die? I wonder if there's any rescues in New Mexico. Hopefully I don't die anytime soon but she just very well may outlive me. That's a problem.

text_content: Some friends thought an ornate box turtle was cute since it was so small and brought it to our house. We called the reptile rescue hospital and they weren't taking any additional exotic animals at this time so we called 3 of the vets that handle exotic pets...and none of them were taking new patients so we did what we thought was best and got him a terrarium, mulch blend, a nighttime heat bulb, a basking bulb, a uvb bulb, a dope skull to hide under, stones for his claws, and some water to dip in and prepare to eat which we feed him earthworms. He's probably a year old now so we're trying veggies. His shell looks great and hes even being good around other people. It was such a challenge and money but it's so worth it. If there's any additional advice someone can give I'd appreciate it.

video_description: My Box Turtle Franklin is the oldest pet I have! At 22 years old I've been caring for him the majority of my life and have learned a ...
text_content: I was given a 3 toed box turtle 35 years ago by a coworker whose daughter was abusing it. She told me it ate wet Purina Cat Chow. I found that even if I put the food out every day, it only seemed interested about once a week so I only put out food once a week after that. Also, I found that since it has free range of the house, it can still sense Summer and Winter and it sort of hibernates in the Winter and doesn't eat. I still have a 2" deep water dish out at all times if it wakes up and needs to hydrate. I had read that the red eyed ones were males and brown eyed ones were females. I got confirmation of that when one day while the food was out and it was basking under the lamp that I put over the food dish. He was "fanning". His junk was all hanging out of his cloaca. It scared the shit out of me but when I got close to check it out, he sucked it all back in. Let me tell ya, there's an awful lot of junk in that trunk. A little research told me what was going on there. Considering what our life is like here, I'd say it is the easiest pet I have ever had. Even in the Summer, I could be gone for a week and no harm done. After 35 years, I must be doing something right. He was full grown size when I got him so I have no idea how old he is. He could drop dread tomorrow for all I know. I may need to get his beak trimmed. It's getting quite long but he can still eat the Cat Chow with no problem.

I wish I was able to identify my old Box Turtle. 
By all appearances you would have thought she was a Northeastern American box turtle but she didn't quite match.
 She had an all dark brown shell with no markings on it, a tan bottom to her shell,  her skin was all tannish lite green with orange spots on her front legs only, and green eyes.

I rescued her years ago at a state park. I was walking along a path and a box turtle came out of the bushes and walked up to me, she tried to climb up my leg wanting to be picked up, when I picked her up she did not suck into her shell, she had all of her extremities hanging out and had her head craned around to look at me. I also will mention that this was the beginning of November when I found her, I found out by an old couple that seen a family had released her into the wild, in the beginning of the winter at that.

text_content: I'm holding my box turtle right now! I've had her for 35 YEARS!  When my nephew was seven, he found and kept her. She was sick and wouldn't eat, so I nursed her back to health and asked if I could have her. Box turtles are a big responsibility. Temperature is very important. If they don't like their conditions, they go on hunger strikes, get weak and die! They need exercise, interaction, and a big aquarium. They are expensive! Canned food, eye drops, shell lotion, heat lamps, reptile bark, special bulbs, big aquariums, vet bills ,etc.  So leave them in the wild or you might have a dead turtle and a sad kid.                                                          That being said ...I love my turtle, and she loves me. She fusses for me to hold her. Plays tug of war and bite the beard.  Likes classic rock music, swims in a big pot for exercise on bath day. I try to hold her for a few hrs every day. Her name is'' Cordelia'' but mainly call her ''Turtle Pants''. A fun pet but not an easy one!                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    PS. .... If you already have a box turtle, I recommend Zoomed box turtle canned food. It's about $3.50 a can (4 feedings) and a steady feeding schedule. My turtle eats every 2 1/2 days.  Mine won't eat dry food. Lettuce doesn't have enough nutrition and bananas is like giving them candy every day. Special box turtle food has fruits, vegetables, fish meal, vitamins, proteins etc.  Also, a bulb that gives both UVA and UVB rays about $30 to $60. Bulbs give them vitamins, so does the eye drops and lotion.  also a aquarium thermometer. and carefully trim the beak and toenails.

text_content: Living so close to the Mississippi here in La Crosse, we occasionally see turtles crossing roads to get to good egg-laying sites further inland, and further into human habitat.  One or two seasons ago, we saw a mom turtle (don't remember if it was a box) heading into our yard from the direction of the river, and then heading back in the other direction a short time later.  I was trying to stay hands-off, heeding wildlife experts' advice that trying to "help" them could get them disoriented, but after a passing motorist tried to "rescue" her from the road by placing her back on OUR side, I decided it would be better to assist after all, and I took her back to the side of the road she was trying to get to.  Hopefully she recognized the terrain and navigated back to the river from there; I don't think there were any other roads in her way.  She did seem to be going the right way before I lost sight of her.

text_content: I have moved multiple box turtles off the road in the direction they were facing- or if that direction looks dangerous, into nearby woods. Recently I stopped for a 6" male that had a 1" piece of shell broken off and hanging. The wound was dirty and festering a little. He was on a super steep dangerous part of Hwy 52 so I brought him home, cleaned and treated the wound, and glued the shell shard back in place with cyanoacrylate. That held nicely, and I am monitoring him to ensure infection doesnt grow. I made him a 6' outdoor pen with food, water, shade, shelter, and plants. He sure sleeps a lot, and I have not observed him eating, so maybe he is sad, or in pain, or both. Soon as the wound heals fully I will release him (back near where I found him if I can find a safe spot). Thanks for an excellent video!

text_content: Fabulous critter! When I was a kid in Northern Virginia in the early 60s, we would see them all the time when we were exploring in the woods. I made a "farm" for them in our backyard to save as many as I could from habitat (and of course their direct) destruction by graders for new housing developments nearby. When we had to move due to my father's reassignment, we relocated our "turtles" (tortoises) to less threatened areas. Interesting factoid, at least in my experience, if you face them into a tree and go off and do your other adventures in the woods, including finding more of them, and come back they'll still be there faced into the tree! I don't think they have much of a sense of reverse! As this video creator suggested, very gentle and even affectionate souls. Tide for my favorite reptile... the other discovered in our eventual move to the Southwest being the desert horned lizard, aka "horny toad"-- same thing, character-ful, gentle souls.

text_content: I recently purchased a baby Eastern Box Turtle and 2 baby Eastern Painted Turtles. The painteds are thriving in a 40 gallon tank right now and spend their days basking, swimming, and foraging on the bottom. One of them likes to hide in the live plants, but the other is much more confident and stays out in the open. The boxie on the other hand is in a terrestrial tank with a Zoomed waterfall, hydroball aquifer, eco-earth/cypress mulch substrate, and reptisun 80w. The boxie ate a few red wrigglers the first few days, but now doesn't eat at all. He ignores them, ignores small spotted roaches, meal worms, pellets, fresh greens and strawberries. He just won't eat. I put him in a plastic tote (no water though) with a red wriggler, spotted roach and meal worm and he just went to a corner. I've dumped about a dozen worms into his tank where they've just went into the soil, along with about 8-10 roaches, and a few meal worms, and none are to be seen now and must've all just burrowed into the substrate. Today, for the first time in 10 days the boxie found the waterfall on his own and stood under the water. So, would it be a good idea to simplify things and put him in a small tub like shown in the video for awhile until he seems to be eating regularly? The one mistake I see I was making after watching your video was not supplying enough water (there was a clay dish filled with water I sat him in a few times and the small water fall stream coming down a bark face and falling onto a gravel bed) and thinking maybe he needed higher temps to stimulate his metabolism. I'm going to raise the height of the reptisun to lower the temp a little (82° on the soil and 92° on the rock were the temps). Also, according to the video, I could also just place him in the 8" deep 40 gallon tank with the similarly sized painteds? He would be just fine swimming and walking on the bottom and using the stairs and floating basking site to get out?

text_content: I had a snapping turtle that was rescued from our parking lot on a hot summer day as a fresh hatchling, about the size of a small coin.  I raised her and handled her every day for years.  Her and I would go all over.  She got so used to people that she was like a puppy, with Petsmart being one of her favorite places.  Usually trying to climb on my shoulder and then from a shopping cart (with padding) when she got too big, trying to get a look at everything.  I had so much trust in her that even when she was big I would try to give her as much free roam time around the house as she wanted.  She usually ended up exploring some then coming back to me, try and dig into my side a bit and fall asleep.  Saying that though, I would never recommend one as a pet to anybody.  Mine was a sweety but it took me years of spending hours with her every day for her to become tame.  And all it takes is 1 bite to mess you up pretty bad for life.

text_content: Hey Tony, just a heads up that red light isnt the greatest for reptiles and amphibians. Just switch over to a different light, your turtles will be grateful!

text_content: Your video just reinforces what I am doing at the front yard. I am creating a butterfly garden enclosure for a future box turtle. I am getting plants in pots that scares away predators such raccoons, possums and cats in the area. Though I would never leave a friend outside alone. I'm very protective of my reptilian buddies. The enclosure is for an hour to two hours of enrichment. Think of it as turtle playground. 😎

I built them a pond and they loved it so much they made baby turtles! They’d never done that before! Three healthy baby turtles emerged after I gathered them from where mama randomly dropped them throughout my living room (we lived in Cancun, Mexico and I’d built a “turtle-proof fence” so they could go from pond to yard to house at will. ) and placed them in soil in a tank. Cutest things you ever saw! Emerging egg-shaped and slowly spreading into dollar-coin sized babies.😊❤

text_content: I regularly saw wild box turtles in creeks and streams when I was younger and explored in the forest a lot.  I also would see them escape into the shallow inlets of the lake if they got startled.  I guess I just never even considered that they couldn't swim because I have always seen them in and around water.  When I kept young box turtles I always kept them in a more swampy enclosure, with both a dry area with a warming lamp and an area with water deep enough to submerge.  Also, I found some of my box turtle's favorite foods to be water plants and small minnows.

text_content: I’m 55 & I often found box turtles as a kid. I had a book when I was little in the 70’s, & I remember it had an illustration of box turtles hibernating under water, & mentioning that a little bit. I used to keep box turtles as pets in the 90’s, & I remember at the time thinking how wrong that book must have been, because at the time the consensus was the box turtles were completely terrestrial, & only would soak in shallow water to drink or defecate. Now I know that that was wrong, & that book I had as a child was correct. I had an Asian box turtle as well, but I kept him in an enclosure that had a large recessed water pan, where he would soak & swim & then a substrate where he would burrow. Now I know that I could have kept him more like a pond turtle with a larger, deeper swimming area, & a smaller area with substrate for basking & burrowing. We live & we learn

text_content:
I had Stubby, an eastern box turtle for 15 years. I learned a lot about turtles from her. She stayed in my room at night & in the morning I carried her out to the pen in the backyard for sunlight & to splash in the pond. One morning, halfway to the back door the phone rang. I sat her down to answer it. (Phones used to be connected to the wall.) After the call, I started doing something else. Ten minutes later I remembered Stubby. Went back to the hallway. No Stubby. She'd walk from the bath to my room, so I figured she went back there. Looked in the closet, under the bed, under the dresser. No Stubby. Checked the adjacent bedroom. No Stubby. The bathroom. Nope. Went thru the dining room. The living room. Nothing. Glanced toward the kitchen & saw a reflection in the leaf of the kitchen table. No, I thought to myself. That's not possible. There, sitting at the back door was Stubby, waiting to go out. That evening, when I brought her back inside, I sat her on the floor by the door & said what I always said when I brought her inside. Time to go to bed, Stubby. And I followed her as she walked thru the kitchen, the dining room down the hall & into my room! All those times I carried her out & in she watched where I went & learned where to go.

In the winter time, she sat by the floor heater to get warm then amble off to her spot under the bed. She loved tuna on whole wheat. She particularly enjoyed the crust. Ate it like a worm. Had ice cream, yogurt, whatever I ate. She loved strawberries, watermelon & yellow apples. Preferred earthworms to meal worms.

When I took her camping, she'd find a cubbyhole in the back of my truck & at night when I rolled out the sleeping bag she'd come in a nestle next to me. It was like sleeping with a cold rock until she warmed up. I was concerned she might suffocate, but next morning she moved out to her niche again. If I put her on a dirt trail, she'd follow my feet. Every few minutes she'd stop & periscope her surroundings. When she did more stopping than walking, I'd carry her back to the truck.

If I sat on the floor, she'd come over & gently pluck at my shorts or shoelaces wanting attention. She loved having her head stroked. 

I miss Stubby. She, too, was one in a million. She passed away in her sleep one wiinter. That was over 30 years ago. 

I've had a lot of turtles in my life & all have been special in their own way. Daisy, a western box turtle, liked to ride on my foot; Touche & Tammy, a pair of eastern box, always laid a clutch in the rose garden & every spring there were five or six hatchlings. Tulip, a western box started the parade of turtles as my first. Rescued Moon, the largest & roundest western box, from being stuck in a redwood fence.  She mated with Pansy & I have photos of her digging a nest & laying a dozen round eggs under the light of the moon & a flashlight. But Stubby showed me the intelligence, love, patience & determination of turtles. I remain hopeful that we will all meet again in that eternal campground & our adventures will continue.

Otis is like a meteor. By happenstance he came into your life. Whoever had him before didn't appreciate him. He knows he is safe with you & decided to show you his true personality! Shine on, Otis. You are indeed special. Moreover, Otis knows he is special, as turtles are very wise.

text_content:
my Russians are currently doing this. the oldest male just doesn't seem to care anymore and walks away  (he's like a million years old and it shows in his attitude), but the 2 youngest males are constantly bumping shells and seem to identify as snapping turtles... it looks brutal, but so far, no blood or visible injuries. I think the smaller male has a bruised ego, though, when he sprints away after a few minutes,  just to return for more within the hour.
before I knew better, I used to constantly separate them, but it only seemed to make the fighting more intense. now I leave them be and once it's feeding time, they are best friends again.

text_content: I had to put a stop to my son bringing turtles home because he  built a turtle pen out back which was fine but when he told me he had over 30 turtles in there I was shocked. He built a pen out of an old trampoline frame and chicken wire and he had a hell of a collection going. He had all kinds of land turtles. Orange spots, yellow spotted ones and plain brown turtles. He basically had all the turtles Kansas has to offer. He had all the turtles tamed to the point he could hand feed them worms and when he would open the gate the turtles would follow him around the yard. There would be a litteral heard of turtles chasing him around. Eventually I talked him into releasing them all back to the woods where he found them but it was hard. Now he goes back into the woods and I kid you not he just has to stand there and a turtle with come crawling up.

text_content: Great video on explaining your feeding set-up.  I'm in Texas and have multiple Ornate's and agree 100% that they should be kept outside.  Hibernation and all.  I was interested in your enclosure walls - they are not electrified and seem to have to "deterrent" for predators.  Where I am, my enclosure is under constant watch by skunks and armadillo's.  They don't want to eat the adults, but dig up my nests.  For the longest time I thought it was a racoon, but after installing night vision video camera's, low and behold, it was skunks and even armadillo's.  I could not figure out how they were getting in and after moving the camera's around and painstakingly watching them (8 hours of nightime video watching is boring....), I finally figured it out and stopped it - but the damage was done.  I used to keep the babies in the main enclosure but exactly like you said, they were getting picked off by hawks.  Again, the vidoe camera revealed all.  So, back my question - no deterants?  Also, do you now attract vermin like rats etc bu leaving ANY food out at night?  I used to and after watching rats and opposums eat the food, I stopped doing that.

video_description: reptile #turtles #lifestyle #boxturtle #boxturtles #reptilecare.
text_content:
Looking to build an outdoor enclosure for eastern boxes. I am located 30 minutes outside of Chicago so winters get fairly harsh.
Ideally, I'd like to keep them outdoors year-round in a fully planted enclosure with a deep enough litter to allow them to go through their natural cycle. Do you have any tips for enclosure specifications? At this moment, I am planning on using cinder blocks for the underground border and going down about 24". I'd back fill with soil mixed with sand to help with drainage.. as we have alot of clay. The burrow area will be a mixture of leaves and pine needles as well logs and go as deep as 18". Let me know what you think, still in planning process. Thanks!

text_content: I grew up a herper in coastal NY this century, and these tales of abundant, easy to find box turtles are torment! A box turtle is the highlight of any day in the field during which one is found. I can count how many eastern box turtles I've seen in NY on one hand. They still seem to be doing okay in the Carolinas, and I've seen ones or twos in NJ and MA... but they're a rarity, and as your host said - very disjunct, occurring only in little isolated pockets.

text_content:
Aggression in male box turtles is very common particularly after emerging from estivation in April (up north.) It’s actually desirable to let many species interact aggressively as they would in the wild just before breeding. I’d allow both my Eastern Box and Spotteds to have at it (under supervision) before mating as it elevates their testosterone levels and stimulates their breeding response. I’ve noticed higher levels of aggression if a female of their species is present (a “show of strength?”) Also, there seems to be a direct correlation with color; the more brightly colored/patterned the male (like Otis) the more dominant/aggressive the turtle. It would be interesting if Chris could breed Otis to see if any of his offspring inherit his “curiosity” traits. 
I noticed for how colorful Otis is his eyes appear to be light orange opposed to the more typical brilliant, fiery red of.  male Easterns. 

*Could Otis be overcompensating due to the “inferiority complex” his pale eyes have given him? In Eastern Box Turtles “eye color matters!”* 😊

text_content:
Yes, those rocky outcroppings, called a _kopje_ (‘copy’), are what pancake tortoises adapted to. They “wedge & inflate” incase they need to escape from predators… because some kopjes are homes for leopards or lion families. Or they’re not, but one day, big cats move in! And a soft, flexible plastron is fun for a lion cub to practice hunting on… lol. So they adapted to wedging themselves.

Here in San Diego, our zoo has an entire Kopje walkthrough exhibit, showcasing the main kopje dwelling species: the klipspringer (antelope), pancake tortoise, dwarf mongoose, rock hyrax, and Sudan plated lizards, all of which are kept together in a large rocky exhibit. The entire thing is basically car-sized, giant granite boulders with hidden burrows in between. Not much actual ground area. Then they also have other kopje-favoring species, including four raptor species (bateleur, martial, Verreaux’s and crowned eagles), servals, and African rock pythons. It’s a great exhibit. Plus on the botanical side, they show all the amazing caudex-forming plants, like _Stephania erecta_ & _Gerrardanthus_ plus succulents like Haworthia, Aloe, Gasteria, Pachypodium, etc.

text_content: I have a horsefield tortoise (or Russian tortoise for you guys in the states) who is just like Otis! If I take him out of his enclosure he will just follow me everywhere and lifts himself up and stretches his neck out as much as he can towards me, and interestingly he NEVER bites me (only my shoes) he's just interested in what I'm doing, but he will constantly try to nip at my girlfriend, so he clearly has preferences. The funny thing is that when I feed him I have to leave the room, even if he can't see me he will not eat while I'm there, he'll just scratch relentlessly against the wall of his enclosure until I pay him attention, so I can only assume he knows my scent, because I've tried just sitting quietly in the room and he still goes nuts as if he knows I'm there. I wish I fully understood why he's so obsessed with me and keen to interact, but I'm not complaining. I personally believe that it's because when we got him he was quite sick - he barely ate or moved and certainly had metabolic bone disease, his shell was pliable and he was super skinny, and I wasn't working at the time due to lockdown so I put all of my time into nursing him back to health. I hand fed him, bathed him in water treated with calcium and vitamins every day, and even gently held up his shell to help him learn how to walk properly until he was strong enough to lift it on his own, so I'm convinced that he somewhat understands that I'm the reason that he's alive and that's why he is the way he is with me.

text_content: We found a tiny turtle 4 years ago on my air force base's flightline.  We  bought a 10 gal tank and kept him enslaved.  He got a bit bigger so we upgraded to a 20 gal tank.  I haven't been able to 100% identify what kind of turtle he is but I'm fairly certain he's a Florida Mud Turtle as we're in Florida.  Somehow he's been living in the shop for over 4 years despite none of us knowing how to properly care for him.  Anyway,  what is the preferred diet for Mud Turtles?  We've tried a great many things but he doesn't seem to eat most of it.  Things he does like are a certain brand of Pleco Wafers for sucker fish, sometimes he eats full sized frozen shrimp. he'll eat Mole-Crickets when we catch them and toss em in the tank.  His absolute favorite was pond snails one of the guys brought in, he ate 45 of them within 2 hours.  Snail-mageddon!  I've bought many different "turtle foods" from amazon but most of them he wont eat.

text_content: While planting an avocado tree in my back yard, I accidentally dug up and damaged a turtle egg. I could see the fully formed guy inside as part of the eggshell had come off. Then I found another egg but this one was undamaged. I brought them inside and kept them moist. The remaining damaged eggshell fell away after a couple of days and there he was, a Florida three striped mud turtle. It's been nearly a week but I can't get him to eat anything. Hopefully soon. I'll have to see if I can find some duckweed. His yoke sack fell off after two days so at least he got some nutrition from that. In the meantime the other egg remains unhatched. This isn't my first rodeo with turtle hatchlings. About three years ago I had accidentally dug up a cooter nest just as they were hatching while I was planting a hibiscus bush. I kept them until they were almost fully grown and then released the three of them into the fresh water canal out behind my house.

text_content: Well done video. I've had a Stinkpot and an Eastern  Mud Turtle (both females) that I was given as adults 30  plus years ago and keep together.  Recorded life spans exceed 50 years. Feeding pellets, algae wafers, a small bit of Medley Treat snacks (dried shrimp, etc.), dew worms (which they love) and very occasionally small aquarium fish.  They also eat a bit of flake fish food. There are a variety of Gourarmis and 2 Corydoras in with them and no nipped fins. However they did eat a school of very small Corydoras in short order.  A word of caution. Stinkpots can climb and escape. Mine has got out a few times in other aqu configurations with lots of plants against the back wall on the land area.  I later found egg clutches hidden behind a stack of magazines on the floor! Basking has been intermittent to non existant over the years but I do provide a heat lamp/UV  basking haul out.

text_content: I love this video and I have one of these guys! It is an adult female in a 40 gallon breeder. I will tell you one thing though with your setup and you may have made one or not but put a screen lid on the tank. I tell you this because when they do bask your dock looks pretty close to the top of the tank. They can escape their tank. My female has gotten out twice. My dock was sitting lower in the tank than yours is and she still got out. It baffles me to this day how she did it. She got out one time and I did not find her for a week and I looked everywhere for her. They are great climbers and in the wild you can see them climbing tree limbs that stick up out of the water and sitting on them to bask. These limbs can have them sitting almost two feet above the water line and when spooked they drop right into the water. It is crazy but they do it. Other than that you have an awesome setup and are taking amazing care of your six stinkpots. 🤙🏼🐢🙂

text_content: Hi, this is Van Gogh’s mom. You reviewed his tank a few months back. He has a lot of natural wood decorations, a natural basking platform, branches and a cave. Those decorations literally cost me almost nothing. I collected the wood (from sycamore trees) as well as rocks and slate from areas near where I live and assembled the decorations with a few screws, silicone and super glue. His tank topper cost $25-$30 for wood and acrylic. He is thriving and when he is not basking spends his time exploring and enjoying his environment. I really appreciate your video’s and this one in particular is right on the mark. So many people get these turtles and just stick them in a tank with some water, feed them , and think they are taking care of them. If you are going to care for one of these creatures please do so responsibly and consider the animals needs first. There is always some expense involved but it does not have to cost a fortune to provide a good home. It does require research, planning and effort and please remember this is a long term commitment. These turtles can live many decades when well cared for. If you are not prepared for that commitment please choose a different pet. If you are committed to providing a proper environment for them they do make amazing and enjoyable pets. And one last note, I often see comments from people saying they found a turtle in their yard or “rescued” one after a storm. Please don’t ever take a turtle from the wild. They are not equipped to live in captivity, they belong in the wild and the wild populations are being lost because of people taking them thinking they are doing the turtle a favor. If you want a pet please get only captive bred turtles. Thank you again for this video.

text_content: Shell care and exercice! I make it a habit to let my turtle out at least 3-4 times a week to make sure he is comfortable walking on land getting good exercice. This is important for him because his tank is basically filled up all the way and his basking area doesn’t allow for too much walking space. I also rub some shell care on him before I let him go, to dry up while he plays. I let him run about outside for about an hour or more, keeping an eye out for birds, lizards, etc. I have a 6 month old YBS and live in Florida, and he’s thriving :)

text_content:
I found this video randomly / on accident(?) / while scrolling. It's a nice video and you have a lot of great information, but I will say it's a LOT to digest. If you make similar videos in the future (videos with a lot of info), then I highly suggest you also add the summarized info in bullet points. You recognize yourself that you're talk fast - and that's fine! Youtube's CC is just... Eh. It was hard for me to stay focused at times, especially since I'm hard of hearing and to really focus on reading both you and the CC to know what you were saying. 

So yeah. I highly recommend also adding the info visually, in the video itself, which is most easily done by just writing the most important stuff in bullet points.
Good luck with your future videos, and stay passionate about the turtles! ♥ You're sharing a lot of great information - now you just have to make it more accessible and easier to digest. :)

text_content:
This specific video came up when i  searched for turtle care. My boyfriend and his family are very ... very ... particular about his turtle. Hes had it since he was a small child and his parents wanted it more than he did, but they also ... dont have much in there. 

Hes been in only water for mostly his entire life. No basking, no decor in the tank, nothing more to eat than the dry food. I bought him some gourmet food like two years ago along with a large rock decor that lets him at least dry out. Its been super hard to try and add things to the tank. Everytime i buy something to add i feel like ive insulted his parents. They try and convince me he needs nothing more than he has and hes been fine for years, and also even tried to get me to return the items i bought but i threw out the reciept so they couldnt. 

 At least he has a massive tank, and a decent filtration system. But im the only one who throws lettuce in for a treat and i feel like everything i try, makes them look at me a certain way. Though, maybe i should be harsher with them about it, i know if this relationship continues to moving in together ill get to take care of the turtle and i look forward to the day in all honesty. Im doing more research right now to see what else i can add to the tank to help the guy, and ill just keep it up. Its hard to convince someone theyve been mistreating a reptile when they dont believe they have been at all since "he seems fine." 

This turned into a rant, but i did want to let you know most of all that for all the videos of yours ive never seen, this will be the first, but not the last. Thank you. You affect the lives of many a turtle in a tank, and i want you to know you will be affecting this one.

text_content: Thank you. This is great information! We have a 10 year old captive bred Stinkpot that we adore. His shell has retained shed flakes that have built up over the past year. We had his water too warm ~79*F and since he never used the basking area we only had a UVA/UVB bulb over the water. He started coming out onto his ramp more and more and we think this was to get out of the water that was too warm. We have the water at 76*-77*F now and have a basking bulb and UVA/UVB light. We were also told in a musk turtle group that all water conditioners are toxic to turtles and we will need drain out the water in his 55 gallon setup (we have it filled about1/3 full) and to use RO or filtered water with no conditioner. We used a new extra soft toothbrush to gently work the retained flakes loose and did a 50% water change with a faucet attachment on the sink for filtered water but it took 4 hours for the water in the tank to come up to temperature because the sink water has to be cold through the filter. What are your thoughts on the safety vs risks of water conditioners such as the Tetrafauna Aquasafe turtle water conditioner or others like it? Thank you

text_content:
Hi, 

Great video. Obviously a very healthy tank, your water quality must be top notch going by the health of the fish. 
I have kept musk turtles for forty five years, the first pair I had , Male & female lived for thirty five years. 
No disrespect to you, but they were kept without a heat lamp or land part to the aquarium. They did have a floating platform available but very rarely used it. The light was a basic tropical aquarium strip light and the base had medium sized pea gravel. I used a heater to keep the water at the correct temperature and a simple air driven box filter. Water changes 50% a week. 
The pair I have now are kept in virtually identical conditions. 
I live on a narrowboat and the last three years I have been out of the country over Christmas for six weeks at a time and turned the heater off. 
I know for a fact that the temperature in the boat would have dropped to nearly zero regularly during this time. 
The turtles went into a mini hibernation and weren’t the slightest bit harmed by this. 
Musk turtles are the only realistic option for average people to keep in aquaria in the U.K. The sliders, soft shells etc grow so enormous it’s difficult to keep up with their requirements. 
Once again , great video, thoroughly enjoyed it, 
Regards warren.

video_title: Turtle Face Off: Stripeneck and Loggerhead Musk Turtles!!
video_description: It's time to compare these two legendary Sternotherus musk turtles; the stripeneck musk (Sternotherus Peltifer) and loggerhead ...
text_content:
My family has had a loggerheaded musk turtle since I was a young kid - back then youtube didn't exist and it was insanely hard to find any information on the turtle. It's actually super-fun to see videos of the turtle and kind of heartwarming to see other turtles of the same type acting like she did when she was young.
I'm quite surprised that you can handle them without getting the ol' bitey-attempts from the turtles, though. The first few years of her life, ours tended to threateningly open her mouth whenever she was held (by the way, she was always placed on a palm, not just held aloft...), and sometimes even try hail-mary attempts to bite where-ever she thought a finger or hand might be. 

Fun fact, we got our turtle when I was 10, and I'm now now 38 years old - which means our silly old turtle-lady is far older then they're supposed to get, at an impressive 28 years old.

text_content:
This tank is massive and absolutely beautiful! Having tank that is less than 10gal/shell length inch is a very common mistake and your turtles are lucky to have tons of room to swim and tons of privacy areas to explore. 

Some suggestions I can provide as a turtle owner (can also find this on r/turtle)
1. Eventually each turtle needs their own tank. As babies, it is ok for them to be in the same tank, but they will get aggressive before fully reaching maturity. Pay attention to neck biting as a clear sign that it is time to separate them.
2. Again with the territorial behavior, it would be better to give each turtle its own basking area and lamp. Even as babies, one of them will start bullying others for the prime basking area.
3. The lamp you had was a single dome so I’m not sure if your bulb is just a heat bulb or a heat/uvb bulb. But turtles also need uvb and people have found that combination bulbs are not reliable at providing uvb. So it would be better to have a dual bulb lamp and have a dedicated uvb bulb to prevent arthritis in your turtles.
4. I saw some suggestions for a pump, but I did see some bubbling in one of your frames so it looks like you have one. Just make sure the pump is 5-10x the volume of your tank. This is another common mistake. So if your tank is 200 gal, you want a pump that can at least pump 1000gal/h. You might have to have multiple pumps in order fulfill that flowrate. 

Hope this helps! It looks like you spare no expense for your pets so I know you will be great turtle dad!

text_content:
To anyone who has or is thinking of getting turtles (including Tony)!! You're gonna need a non-moving land space about 25% of the tank area (like a platform they can swim under with ramp) with a uv bulb (replaced every year) and heat lamp above it, and a feed bowl for foods (including veggies) dusted with calcium and vitamins, otherwise they can get some really bad deformities.

A good rule i like to follow is even if you think you know everything you need to about your animals, do lots of googling and forum-searching before getting, and every couple months do another google to see if there's anything you could do to make their life any better. That helps avoid any mistakes, and leads to peace of mind and happy animals😊


E.g. tank size requirements for 3 adult _species_ turtles, _species_ turtle diet, treats, how often to feed, adult size, etc. Whatever pops in your mind, or scroll random forums if you can't think of anything

video_title: MY $10,000 TURTLE TANK 🐢
text_content: Hey man, turtle guy here. Nice tank man really great size, most people unfortunately keep them in tanks way too small for them. But a few pointers. Its actually not healthy for them to eat too many goldfish or minnows due to an enzyme they carry. Guppies and swordtails are a healthier livefood. Next careful with the gravel cause if it can fit there mouth you risk them choking or swallowing it so I suggest play sand, which you can get a 10-20 pound bag from home depot for like $5 you just have to clean it well, or large river stones. Lastly turtles can get very territorial so if you are planning on getting more turtles they're probably gonna need their own tank rather than living all together in one. Favorite species are Red Eared sliders and Diamondback Terapins tho. Great tank and start again tho. I know you're gonna spoil them and I can't wait to see updates.

text_content: Feeding goldfish and Rosie's will eventually kill your turtles. They have a chemical called thiaminase which will build up in the turtle's system and when it gets bad it will display as neurological issues with the turtles. It blocks vitamin B12, and a heavy dose of B12 can fix it if noticed soon enough. My advice is to Google fish that don't contain thiaminase and use those, you can even buy frozen fish, thaw them out and chop them up and the turtles won't see any difference. Also I love how you did an elaborate setup for very common turtles, you're putting people to shame! Many species need different types of cages or temperatures so please keep that in mind. My side job is reptiles in general, monitor lizards and snakes (dozens of species) if you ever need more specific info hit me up. video_description: So you think you want a pet turtle, but you aren't sure which turtle is right for you. Clint looks over the pros and cons of five of the ...
text_content:
This was an amazing introduction to the different types of tortoises and turtles out there, but also because you talk about responsibility in the first few minutes! I own a Russian (Morty the Tortie) and a Red-footed (Yurtle the Turtle) and respect them both so much as amazing reptiles! Russians don't need just a clear enclosure to not understand boundaries. Our Morty is in a dark enclosure and when he wants to get out for a walk, will repeatedly bang, scratch, and try to climb out. One thing I think you could have hit on with the Russian is that they need lots of exercise and/or a nice big enclosure, because in the wild they impressively walk 4 MILES A DAY (pretty impressive when you take their size into account!) Their also speed demons! My guy can run off in a flash, so you have to be careful to not lose sight of them. 

A big pro is their amazing personalities! Morty the Tortie reminds me of a grumpy old man who is actually lovable under their hard shell! He's very social and so smart! They're amazingly great pets, but just like you, I wish pet stores would not sell wild caught and teach buyers that they need a big enclosurer of about 6 square feet. 

Thanks so much for teaching responsibility in reptile ownership, something rare for channels because they are also breeding them. Also, I LOVE that you spoke about rescuing sulcatas. Amazing video! I subscribed instantly!

text_content: I found a painted turtle on the golf course in spring 2021. It was in the middle of the fairway, bone dry, and the size of a bottle cap, so I put him by the nearest water, over 200 yards away. Came back 20 minutes later and he was still there, in it's shell. I took it home as my 55 gallon tank was almost done cycling, and now he's healthy and well, although missing two toes when I found it, the turtle is a great swimmer. I finally ordered a mercury vapor bulb, as I can't let him bask naturally in the winter for the cold, and lack of sunlight in Michigan. I can't believe my turtle is still alive and I hope it's got years left in the tank!

text_content: I currently have 13 baby painted turtles.  They randomly came up onto my porch, and I collected them.  I do not live that close to water, so I do not know where they came from.  They are happily residing in a large plastic drawer turned pond with natural rocks, plants, and a filter.  Their habitat is distilled water, and they are fed ReptoMin plus natural greens.  They are currently basking and SO cute.  I am not utilizing UV lights or heaters because, obviously, they do not have that in the wild.  I keep them by the window. Which is always open to some degree despite the weather.  They get natural variations in sunlight.  All are hardy and strong.  Hopefully, I can find them good homes.

video_title: I released SNAPPING TURTLES into my pond…
text_content:
If anyone wants to find turtles in any pond just take any piece of floating wood, even a non-treated wood plank about 2 x 10 and over 4 ft long + , wrap a inexpensive metal chain (or even nylon rope) around center of the wood or plank and put a cement cinder block (or anything heavy) attached to the other end of the chain. And then do your best to place it in the Center of the pond and within month you will see ALL of the turtles basking on the plank / wood.  Almost all turtles, including snapping turtles, need / love to bask in the Sun if they feel it's in a safe area, not near the bank or other near dangerous animals.
  Then you can watch from the bank and count how many and what types are in the pond.

text_content: So depending on the uvb light you have it may need to be closer to his basking spot. Your plants may not do very well in just gravel. I have a nutrient layer cover with a layer of sand in my snapping turtles tank. Turtles love to dig in the sand as well. I nutrient layer under the sand is in substrate bags so he doesn't dig it up. Because turtles can be pretty dirty I would gravel vac and do regular 50% water changes regularly. I keep mosquito fish with my turtle as well as ghost shrimp and small snails. They are enrichment, janitors, and food if he chooses. He is a good swimmer but because he's a baby and can get tired pretty fast, keep a close eye on him in water that deep. Not trying to be an internet professional, just some advice from another turtle keeper.

text_content: Ok, out of kindness here is some turtle care needs and warnings for keeping turtles, I looked though the comments and noticed some people had outdated or incorrect turtle care recommendations and stuff. I just need people to understand this before getting turtles because I had read every comment and I do not want anyone getting turtles and unknowingly putting them at risk. And the one thing that bothered me the most in the comments is “you should get more turtles” or “Get him a friend “ like idk if you guys know but NO! 👉TURTLES👏 ARE 👏NOT 👏SOCIAL👏 ANIMALS 👏AND 👏THEY 👏WILL 👏HAVE 👏A 👏HIGH 👏POSSIBILITY 👏FOR👏 HARMING👏 IF 👏NOT 👏KILLING 👏EACH 👏OTHER! 👏IF 👏YOU 👏WANT 👏MORE👏 THEN 👏ONE 👏TURLE 👏IN👏THE👏SAME👏TANK👏THEN 👏YOU 👏WOULD 👏NEED 👏A 👏LARGER 👏TANK! 👏AND 👏EVEN 👏THEN 👏THERE 👏WOULD👏 ALWAYS 👏HAVE 👏A 👏RISK 👏OF 👏THEM👏BEING 👏HURT 👏OR 👏DYING! 👈 I’M NOT SAYING TO BE RUDE, I AM SAYING THAT TO PREVENT ANY IMPULSE BUYING THAT WOULD LEAD TO A HURT OR DEAD TURTLE AND PREVENTING A NEW OWNER FROM LEARNING THE HARD WAY THAT CAN BE HEARTBREAKING! 💔 So if you get a turtle the general rule for aquatic turtles is 10 gallons of water per inch of shell length, meaning a 4-inch turtle needs a minimum 40-gallon tank, and adults often require 75-120+ gallons; PUR TURTLE, DOUBLE THAT AMOUNT FOR TWO THOUGH I WOULD NOT RECOMMEND HOMEING THEM TOGETHER! this provides enough swimming space and accounts for their growth, but remember it's a minimum, and larger is always better. Always have a basking area that a turtle can get completely dry on and with that they need heat and UVB, preferably not the conjoined double lamp heat and UVB because the UVB light  in those kits aren’t the best, instead a tube UVB light that’s a 5T light is more recommend and make sure both the heat and UVB light hit the same basking area, also a water filter is also important and because turtles are messy, I say for example if you have one baby turtle in a 20 gallon long tank get a filter that is at least met for a 40-55 gallons (that what works for me at least with the tiny baby turtle) and as your turtle grows they will need to be upgraded so if you wanna save on money look into buying second hand tanks or using storage tubs or stock tanks, you don’t even have to fill the tank up all the way if it’s still a baby, you can start with a 75 gallon tank for example and keep it shallow and just raise the water level as it grows (keep in mind the water should be deep enough for it to swim but shallow enough to easily reach the surface to breathe, typically 1.5 to 2 times its shell length, or about 4-8 inches, gradually increasing as it grows stronger, ensuring it can always stand on its hind legs and touch the bottom or easily get to the surface.) and as for plants it depends on the turtle, I have some turtles that would ignore the plants (like my Mississippi map turtle) who would eat the romaine lettuce and sliced carrots but won’t eat the guppy grass in his tank or the hornwort. Or my baby red belly cooter who would eat every plant I give her besides the hornwort. Turns out turtles in the wild don’t care for hornwort do to the texture of the plant even if it’s safe to eat. None of my turtle eat or even like hornwort so you can try hornwort if you like just keep in mind it’s a floating plant,doesn’t need substrate. Also when it comes to substrate there is many options to choose from, you can do sand, tile, no substrate, large river rocks (make sure they are too big for the turtle to eat) but I would stay away from aquarium gravel because it can cause  impaction if eaten and there is many bad stores of turtles dying from aquarium gravel. As for decoration make sure that it’s something that would NOT crush the turtles, something that wouldn’t risk the turtle getting stuck, something that does not have blade like edges,and something that is aquarium safe that the turtle won’t eat, sometimes people use plastic plants but for me personally I wouldn’t trust it because I heard some turtles nip at it thinking it’s a real plant at first and we wouldn’t want our beloved turtles to eat plastic. Another thing is food, do NOT feed turtles  goldfish, rosy red minnows(any any minnows),and other cyprinids (like common minnows, dace) away from turtles because they contain thiaminase, an enzyme that destroys Vitamin B1, causing neurological damage, paralysis, and death in turtles, and also avoid aggressive fish like Cichlids that can harm the turtle's eyes. Slow, colorful, or long-finned fish like Bettas, Koi, or large Plecos are also bad choices as they are easy prey or attract unwanted attention, while fast, small livebearers (guppies, mollies, platies, and mosquito fish) are better options for enrichment or occasional treats, but always monitor for safety. Also turtles need a water heater, even more so in their baby and juvenile year! As for main food pellets there is many options just try and keep it diverse and try to avoid as much of the low quality ones if possible, for me personally I use Ultra Fresh Floating Turtle food, zoo med turtle food, and mazuri turtle food. Also almost forgot but make sure you had a cuddle bone for calcium,turtles need it for their shell health. ANYWAY Please make sure to do your research before getting a turtle let alone any pet, turtles are a big commitment and can live for many years! And if one day you end up not being able to care for the turtle do NOT release it into the wild, turtles brought up in captivity can not survive and if they do they can become invasive in some areas, best to responsibility find the turtle a new home if one day you decide you don’t want it or can’t care for it anymore!

text_content: While hibernating bears don't really sleep all winter, painted turtles are capable of totally burying themselves in the mud at the bottom of their ponds all winter and entering a state of almost suspended animation called brumation -- absorbing oxygen through their cloaca is one of the reasons they can do this. I've noticed that painted turtles and mallard ducks are indeed generally very tolerant of one another -- turtles resting on mats of vegetation just below the surface usually arent bothered by ducks swimming over and around them, and both species seem willing to share space near each otner on a warm rock. This might be because mallard ducks tend to form flocks, and while turtles aren't very social animals,  painted turtles have a particularly strong propensity for basking on and around each other,  sometimes even forming vertical stacks of 3-5 turtles when basking space is limited.  Also, based on their physiology, those two painted turtles are both females -- males have very distinctive long claws on their front feet. They're not really used for hunting, fighting, or digging -- they're mainly for performing their mating display,  and for stroking the females' heads as part of courtship behavior.
