text_content: For my 15 year old slider the rule of thumb has always been the deeper the better. Now let's remember Red eared sliders can get pretty big compared to when they were babies so the water depth just keeps getting deeper and deeper as they grow. My slider is full grown now and is pretty happy in a four foot water depth, loves to hang out and sleep on the bottom. I could even go deeper if I wanted too but she is happy with four feet so I'm happy with four feet lol. I just make sure she has a good area to climb out and bask in if she want, I keep a heat lamp over her basking area and she uses it when she wants. When my slider was a baby and tiny she was in a tank two feet deep and had no problems with that loves to dive down to the bottom. My only experience with turtles is this one turtle, got it for my son when he was 10 years old and my son is now 26 years old. I guess my 15 year old turtle is actually a 16 year old turtle lol. Remember turtles can live for a very long time, be sure to take that into consideration before getting one.

I just went to the vet and they confirmed that he had been fighting a respiratory infection. He showed no signs of heartbeat, lung activity, or head and eye movement. He had everything that he needed including lighting, filtration, water heating, and was being fed every single day. I was hoping to see him to at least 1 year but I guess he could not battle the infection anymore. Thank you so much for all of these helpful videos.

I used to be really depressed, and I got him for a way of mental support, but it didn't work out so well when I found out a good filter costs 100 dollars. I bought one filter that I could say, was doing its job really well, but the problem is that my tank water was really small, the capacity was only around 7 gallons. I used to keep my 5 inch turtle in a 7 gallon tank, that was not filled all the way up because it could've easily escaped.(keep in mind I didn't have a lid). I kept my turtle in that exact same tank for one year, the water was like 2 inches tall. It was a really bad life for my turtle. 
Some months ago, I saw my turtle refuse to eat, walk around or even sit on the magnetic island that couldn't keep his weight anymore, and when he'd sit, the island would sink. I got the sign. There was something wrong about the way I was treating him. I used to not feed him, or feed him rarely. I knew I had to do something, even though I had not even close to enough money. I couldn't ask my friend for money, because they are barely surviving, getting paid one dollar per hour and I usually give them a visit and order food while I'm there. (they are disabled and no workplace would accept them) So I got a job. At starbucks. Not the best paid, but it was good enough to save around 300 dollars to get everything my turtle needs. In 2 months, I had the money. With a lot of luck, my turtle survived because I cleaned the aquarium more often now, I fed him and I gave him conditions for him to survive. 
Today was the day my turtle got the biggest present of his life. He got a 35 gallon tank, 31 inch wide and 15 inch tall. I filled it up all the way with tap water, and now I'm letting it sit for around a week(I have two reasons for that. 1: tap water is not safe,) And the second reason is that I ordered some really nice fake plants, a water filter with activated charcoal, a lid for my tank with led lights and a big island. I don't know if the tank is big enough, but it sure is better than before. My turtle will feel so much better, and probably get used to me.
I will watch more of your videos and other turtle owner content creators so I'd see how to take care of turtles.

text_content: Those UVA+UVB light combo are completely inadequate for turtles, they don't make enough UVB at all and that shows on the deformed looking turtle shell. Try Googling Red Eared Slider in nature and you can see the shell look different. You need two separate lights for basking, one UVB 10.0 and one heat. Also the basking area must be totally dry, in your case it sinks a bit and the plastron can not dry properly.

text_content: I have two Red Eared Sliders. They are actually siblings. Their mother made her nest in my yard two years ago. I decided to keep just one. I decided to move their tank into my office, so they would get used to seeing me all day. Now, they don't run when I enter the room.  I love just them!

video_title: 6 Things to Know BEFORE You Get a Red Eared Slider Turtle
video_description: In this video, I discuss what it's like to have a red-eared slider as a pet. While red-eared slider turtles are great, there are a couple ...
text_content:
I am 70 yrs old and live in a mobile home park with a very large pond (football field) in the middle, well it went toxic last November ck, filled with foam and bacteria, years of poop and such, we also have a lot of ducks. Park tried to deal with, but out of their league and too cheap to hire pros, long story short is they all started dying, we had probably 100 sliders, up to 20-30 yrs old.  It was killing me watching them, die such slow deaths.  

I finally started rescuing the ones that were visibly were sick, gave them ABX shots etc. with the help of an online vet from ND (I am CA) saved 30 out of 34 and rehomed privately., Fast forward 4-5 months, came the babies.  The first round cost a lot of money and was hard on me for the 6 weeks nursing them back to health. Well, all my senior friends who walk around the pond with their dogs, first brought me 1 baby, then 2-3-4.  I kept saying please no more, I do not have the $$, capacity or strength to raise them to 4" (my heart is too sensitive) when I can adopt them out to a reptile store... BUT I can't turn them down either, they were the size of a quarter and pond still toxic.  

1 died after 4 months, I have no idea why I tested water almost daily food, basking etc. all checked. His name was Only (at the time the "only" one who made it) he was doing great and then just died on my 70th birthday, I am very sensitive to helpless creatures, and it wrecked me.  That's when the others started coming and now have 3.  

One look like she's (guessing) on steroids she's growing so fast and the other isn't growing as fast, probably male, they are in a 30-gallon tank together even though they say not to, they are best buds, the smaller rides on the bigger ones back.  Then came, 3 more recently and I had a 10 gallon that I already had bought for "Only".  I know I need bigger tanks, but I cannot afford nor have the room to go any bigger.  

Too small to adopt out in CA as it's illegal. The all of a sudden, the bigger one (Chance) was very lethargic, eyes looked swollen, (possibly upper respiratory), so I freaked out and took them both to animal ER which was a joke and "very" expensive. Then they seemed fine again, now Chance is doing it again, he won't bask, stays hanging on the water lettuce plant all day, BUT she is eating and swimming. I am at my wits end and emotionally it's killing me because I don't have the vast knowledge I need. I have a lot but they're still too small to give ABX shots safely. Every single item I read on internet, I am doing. 

If anyone can give me some advice it would be greatly appreciated.  This is a full-time job, and I am not that strong to lift tanks to completely clean, I have to get someone over to do that part. Water changes I am fine with and again water temps, food, cleanliness, etc. all CHECKED❤❤!  PLEASE HELP ME, I don't want to lose Chance, Sassy and Little one (name for now).😢😢 Thank you!

text_content: I got my red eared sliders from my brother, who got them from a garage sale held by our mother's friend. text_content: I have an around 19 year old res named kachu(punjabi word for turtle) that was gifted to me on my 3rd birthday. ashamed to say i didnt really care for her as children do and my parents basically treated her like a dog when she outgrew her tank. She spent a lot of time on the concrete floor of our backyard and fed regular fish pellets in water in a shallow basin that's kind of built into the floor, barely enough to submerge her fully. I'm turning 18 in a few days now and somehow gained consciousness ten years too late so ive pushed for her to be fed veggies and meats(boiled in water with no salt) along with some mildly expensive turtle pellets that she seems to like. she's nesting in my grandpa's garden now, even laid a single egg(i think she'll lay more, last time she laid around eight) and we're having a 1 meter x 2.5 meter x 0.5 meter pond built for her in the backyard, along with a dirt and grass patch to see if she stops eating all the plants my grandpa painstakingly planted. This video is a great help, and I sincerely hope to God that she forgives me for treating her so badly for half my life 😢

text_content: We had one growing up, but not for the typical reason. My dad was offered a RES that had a 4 inch shell size from someone who didn't want it anymore. He set up a tank in his own personal office and meticulously cared for it for years until it grew too big to be comfortable for it. He then dug a pond in the back yard for it, which it stayed in for another decade, well cared for. After he'd had it for at least twenty years he had a local biology teacher offer a very good setup for it (I believe the carapace was 8-9 inches though at that point) with the promise of giving it back should they no longer have a home for it. I believe it is still alive at almost 30 years right now!

text_content: I got my Red Eared Slider Turbo when she was approx 1-2 years old, from a cousin who didn't want her anymore. That's her, my icon picture. She had to have an x-ray. I had no idea what I was in for, but I wouldn't trade her for the world. She's been with me now for 17 years, most of my life. She lives in a 110-gallon Tuff-Tub, for which I have custom-built a landing platform and heat and light rig for her. Full-spectrum + UVB + ceramic heat emitter + a canister filter rated for a 500-gallon outdoor pond. Here's what they don't tell you: when you have a turtle this big they don't make habitat supplies for you. You have to get very comfortable thinking outside the box, breaking out the power tools, and making late-night trips to your local hardware store sometimes. But I love my baby! She's smart, stubborn, the vet always complements her on her beautiful carapace, and she's afraid of precisely nothing. She's the perfect fit for me as a companion animal.

text_content: I appreciate that you pointed out that there are plenty of these turtles already in need of homes. One thing I wish you had added was the reason why these turtles are actually illegal in some places. Here in the Pacific Northwest, red eared-sliders are invasive, competing against our native painted and western pond turtles. People will either intentionally let their pets loose, or they will put their pets in a pond or stream in their yard, and it's not too hard for them to find their way to other bodies of water. Like you said, they are really hardy reptiles, which unfortunately makes them really good at establishing invasive populations, so it is important for people to be aware of just how hardy (and potentially harmful) they can be.

video_title: Red-Eared Slider, The Best Pet Turtle?
video_description: The red eared slider (Trachemys scripta elegans) is probably the most popular aquatic turtle available today. They are beautiful ...
text_content:
Awesome video, as usual! I'm glad you heavily emphasized the difficulty of keeping/affording these turtles, because my male RES is by far my most expensive pet I have (and that says a lot, as I have fish and aquascaped aquariums.)  To add on a little more useful information:
- I'd definitely cycle the filter in advance (like with any fish tank, really). This will take 4-8 weeks, depending on how fast the beneficial bacteria, namely Nitrosomonas and Nitrobacter, are able to establish in the biological media. This will remove all ammonia and nitrites from the water and convert them to nitrates. Ammonia can and will burn your turtle's eyes, and even skin, but nitrate is much less toxic and gets changed out with weekly water changes.
- Cohabbing turtles is pretty common, but a lot of the time it's not a good idea. You'll need a lot more space if you want to keep 2+ turtles, and you'll have to have a backup tank if things go wrong. Usually, turtles will be okay together as hatchlings and suddenly become quite aggressive as juveniles/adults, so just because they're okay together as babies does not mean they'll live happily together as adults. If you do want to house multiple adults together, you'd be best off with only females or at most one male and females, as multiple males together is too risky. You'd also need a lot more space, preferably a pond if you want 3+. Also, when cohabbing males and females there's the chance of ending up with hatchlings, which you have to deal with.
- Aquariums are expensive. So, if you don't care too much about aesthetics, I'd recommend a stock tub as they're built to hold water and are much less expensive. I got a 150 gallon from Tractor Supply for $115 for my male RES. It doesn't look as nice as a glass tank, but a glass tank of that size can run you $800-$1500 dollars.

video_title: Red-Eared Slider, The Best Pet Turtle?
video_description: The red eared slider (Trachemys scripta elegans) is probably the most popular aquatic turtle available today. They are beautiful ...
text_content:
I had a red-eared slider for about 15 years. My parents bought him (and about 3 others who didn't make it past the first month) for us at a farmer's market. I knew nothing about taking care of a turtle and did not at the time think to look for resources to better take care of him.

Over the years I got a bit better with him, but when it came time to move 600 miles, I realized that I wasn't giving him the home that he needed, and donated him to a local reptile care facility. With any luck he's living a much better life now.

The point of me saying all that is, I wish that I could have seen a video like this before getting my turtle. I would have known either to not get him at all or that I was in for a lot of work, which I ended up finding out the hard way.

Now that I'm working towards my first Ball Python, I am so, so grateful for channels like Clint's that help us learn about what it takes to take care of these wonderful animals. I hope that this video helps others learn much more easily than I did that red-eared sliders, which deserve to have great lives, are not the easiest pets to give a great life.

text_content: There’s a lot of misinformation here. Especially in the photos and videos shown. There’s a couple turtles with severe metabolic bone disease (shells curling up). Pictures of turtles being offered food out of water (they have to eat in water or they will choke). There is a photo of a turtle with a severely overgrown beak. Many of the photos/videos you show are not even red eared sliders. You responded to someone’s comment about a serious size difference in their 2 red eared sliders. It’s not a good idea to house more than 1 together because they will compete for resources and one could be winning (the dominant one). That’s not everything wrong with the video. Please don’t put out animal care videos if you don’t do the proper research.

video_title: Two headed turtle Update‼️
text_content:
Good evening.  I am so  glad your patient(s), survived the ordeal of the past few days/week.  I’ve got some gen. tips.  First, I am a retired animal keeper of a rescue zoo in PA.  Over my tenure of 30 + yrs., I’ve served as head keeper of all the depts., (domestics + exotics), mainly w/  birds, mammals and reptiles.  Anytime you put a turtle, baby or adult, in a container, please know that even a weak critter will try to escape, it gets them into real trouble as you’ve now experienced.  Set your container up to replicate nature, they will feel more that it’s their home.  We set our baby turtle tanks up w/ a  wet part and a dry part, doing that replicates nature.  Add some rocks they can scratch on, a few sticks and greens for them to eat, like duck weed, etc.  if using a plastic tank Start by scuffing
 the inner bottom of your smooth, of plastic, container, it provides traction for their nails to catch on to right themselves.  

It would be better to not house  turtles  on a plain, smooth tank bottom, they need enrichment in their habitat, like they’d have in nature, they live near and in fresh water ponds, they haul out during the day to sun themselves.  Gather together several pounds to of local dirt & clay, then use the clay  for a bottom layer, across the entire container.  Mix the dirt & clay together, to make a higher level and an even higher mound for 3;4 of the container.  It’s been our experience the 2 head turtles always want to do different things, even fight the other head, constantly flipping itself over, as you’ve seen.  Enriching the enclosure will help them from boredom and searching for a real pond.  To keep babies safe we use a piece of wood, a 1” x 6” x width of enclosure, of untreated wood, we use the board to divide the water side from the dry side, when they are unattended by humans, never leave them unattended on the water side.   We soak daily 10;15 min, several times a day.  After our babies are done soaking, swimming, etc., we put in the dividing board, to prevents accidents in case one flips another over or themselves over in the water,.  baby Turtles can drown, even in 1/4” water.   Also, turtles need plenty of Sun or, reptile light source, be careful though, if your glass tank is in front of a window, the Sun can overheat them fast, use a shade cloth if tank is in direct Sun or in front of a window, especially in Summer with Southern exposure.  

Soak them instead, but never leave them alone in any water.  At their stage offering water via dish is tricky for a  turtle with 2 heads, if you add a small water dish bury it in the dirt mound so it is is flush with the dirt level.  We use a teeny syringe, to offer water that way we can monitor each heads water intake.  Also a wet rag in a corner of tank is helpful, they can mouth it to moisture, while you are away.  Your turtle can also get water from foods you feed like crushed fish, plants, greens, damp turtle pellets, etc.  it’s a good idea to weigh your turtle weekly.  Good luck to you on this journey, the 2 headed turtle can be difficult to manage.   If you need help, there’s many online success stories, don’t hesitate to reach out to the folks keeping duel headed turtles alive and thriving.

text_content: Story time for anybody interested:I saved a turtle's life, so 45 minutes ago, my sister was riding her scooter around the street and she found a very small turtle, on the side of the road, and for context, the nearest pond is a 45 minute walk there, it was roughly the size of the palm of my hand, we had a bunch of cats so I didn't want to risk the cats messing with the poor lil guy, so me and my sister went to the pond near the park and as I held it, I think it started to get used to me as it was moving slowly, when I set it down near the water, it took it a few moments before it went into the water, burying under some seagrass, still trying to process that incredible moment, I'm not sure if it came from the pond of if it was someones pet they either lost, hopefully not abandoned, but I will forever cherish that moment.

text_content:
Great tips for sure! There are a couple things to keep in mind:
1. Hand feeding may make the turtle associate your hand/presence with food, and thus they will grow a heavy dependence on being hand fed. My turtle will sometimes ignore food in the tank unless I touch it or have him follow with my finger. Now, I just drop the food and let him find it to make sure his natural hunting instincts develop.
2. Feeding a hatchling a snack for doing certain behavior may cause over feeding if done too often. Hatching turtles will always act hungry and welcome food whenever given, so balancing their meal portion with a snack portion is very important. Remember, a hatchling turtle should be fed the volume of food that fits the volume of their head daily, no more! Their shell may start pyramiding and gain weight too quickly. 
Hope this helps anyone!

video_title: Rescued Turtle Update 🐢 #pets #animals #aquarium #wildlife #nature
text_content:
My turtle has grown a lot since the day he came to me 5 months ago. I have named him SIKKA because he was coin sized when I first saw him and also because he was strong enough to survive the conditions which dozens of turtles couldn't, at a wholesaler's place.

Below are certain tips that I have written down from my own experience (not from the internet):

• Big enough setup (not too deep if the turtle is small) Do not install high flow filter. Do not use small tubs and readymade plastic enclosures that are too small and fancy.
• Dry area (where it can climb and completely dry off) with turtle lamp above it to provide heat. It should he on for atleast 8 hours everyday. Turtles spends a lot of time in that area. You can search "Turtle Lamp" and "Turtle Platform" on any online shopping platform.
• Direct morning sunlight everyday for 1-2 hours in a small container, put 1-2 cup of water in it so that the turtle doesn't dehydrate.
• Install aquarium heater in the setup during winters. Set the heater at 24 degrees.
• Add small pieces of Turtle Calcium Block everytime you change water. 
• Give Saki Hikari Turtle food everyday, worms and insects sometimes as well. You should also feed boiled spinach, grated carrot or coriander 2-4 times a week. You can also feed a small piece of apple or banana once a week. When they are small, they need high protein to grow properly and as they age, they need more vegetable matter. Do not feed the turtle anything once every week, fasting is important too.
• Clean the tank once or twice a week. Clean water is very important to avoid shell rot, eye infections and respiratory problems.

text_content: keeping water turtles is really really a lot of work. Keeping their water clean. I had 3 water turtles. i got them from ponds as hatchlings. one RES and 2 Eaatern Painted turtles. The one painted turtle was so small it would fit down the neck of a soda bottle. Probably would not have survived in the wild. they were ok till they got around 2 -3 inches. Even with a zoo med filter i still had to syphon off crud and about 1/3 of the water in a 20 gallon every other day. maybe i was feeding them too much. You also need a UV-B lamp so the turtles can metabolize calcium. Forget about sunlight coming through a window. Most window glass has UV blocker. If you want your turtles to see in color you will need a UV-A lamp as well.  you can give them all the calcium you can w/o UV it's wasted. Get a mealworm colony going in some oatmeal, feed a whole fish, internals and all. NO HAMBURGER, too rich for them. also try lettuce and greens. IMO RES are mean turtles. Painted turtles are friendly turtles. one pond i went to I saw about 10 painted turtles playing follow the leader, all the dips and dives, turns the other painted turtles did following. A quarry i swim in there was a painted turtle playing with young boys. the turtle would appear at the surface a few feet away and the boys laughing would swim towards the turtle, at the last moment the turtle would disappear only to pop up in back of them they would turn laughing and the process started all over again.

text_content:
FUN FACT! Blue is the hardest color to form in nature. It's been known to form from pigmentation in only 2 organisms and is rather caused by the structure of other organisms outer coating. So for things that don't rot, like feathers and shells, any blue will stay as vibrant due to the color not relying on nutrients to maintain it.
EDIT: fact checked
EDIT: Since so many keep commenting blue animals let me clarify. 
Only 2 animals make blue PIGMENT. Meaning that the body works to create the color. 
On the other hand every other blue creature uses PHYSICS to create blue. There are a few ways to do this but this means that the body doesn't create PIGMENT to make the color blue.

text_content: I am in Michigan and keep radiata. I diapause eggs at around 64 degrees for 30 days, then keep them at room temp of about 73 degrees for around 24 hours before placing them in an incubator. If the incubator temp is at 86-87 degrees, they usually hatch in around 90 days, taking up to another 30 days if the temp is lower at around 84-85 degrees. For diapause, the eggs are on dry vermiculite, then on moistened vermiculite when placed in the incubator.

text_content:
Just saying but I think you should get angonoka, Pancake, Bolson, homes hinge back, impressed, leopard and spider tortoises and these include some of my favorites along with others and the angonoka belongs to the same genus as the raidiated tortoise. 
But the pancake tortoise is one of my favorites and they are called pancake tortoises because they’re name refers to the flat shape of its shell. 
The Bolsons tortoise is a kind of tortoise that lives in North America and out of the six species of North American tortoises it is the largest with a carapace length of about 46 centimeters(18 inches).
The Home’s Hinge back tortoise is a kind of tortoise from Africa and it’s named in honor of English surgeon and naturalist Sir Everard Home 1st baronet and Sir Everard Home is best known for  first describing a large extinct marine reptile called an Ichthyosaur in the first scientific publication.
The Impressed tortoise is a relative of the Burmese mountain tortoise but the males are smaller.
The Spider tortoise is a kind of tortoise found on the island of Madagascar and they are called Spider tortoises not because they look like spiders but because of their web like pattern of yellow lines on its dark brown or black carapace or its shell. 
The last kind of tortoise I think you should get is called a leopard tortoise and it’s a large and attractively marked tortoise that can be found in the savannas of eastern and Southern Africa and it’s a grazing kind of tortoise and in the wild when the weather gets very cold or very hot it will dwell in abandoned fox, jackal and aardvark burrows and they are called leopard tortoises because of the leopard like rosettes on their shell. 
And just saying but if you do get the pancake tortoise I think that you’re son cash would like it because of how small they are but also because of how fast they are and they are also an agile climber and they are rarely found far from rocky homes and they are found not far from their rocky homes because if disturbed they can make a dash for the nearest rock crevice and since they can be easily torn apart by predators it relies on its speed and flexibility to escape from dangerous situations rather than withdrawing into its shell and the pancake tortoise is also unique in that it exploits and takes advantage of the narrow rock crevices which no other tortoise can use.

text_content: Kenan, 40 years of turtle and tortoise keeping has taught me that some just eat weird stuff.  I have watched them intentionally eat rocks and mulch.  I used to think they ate rocks as some primitive digestive aide, similar to birds, but I don't know.  I do know that some develop odd habits or behaviors much like humans do and stick with them.  Maybe they're expressing their own uniqueness - who knows.

text_content:
Kenan I've used dog kennel trays for years with my tortoises. Its shallow enough for small torts to get into but deep enough to stop any spillage. I also use dough proofing trays for water dishes on my larger torts. I bury them so  the tray is flush with the ground. That way it can't be flipped over if the tort steps on the side of the tray. For hatchlings I use bird cage trays because they are super shallow and baby reds and sulcatas can easily walk in an out. 
Hope this helps and sorry for your loss.

text_content: We are caring for a southwest desert tortoise for a couple more weeks. He has gotten very restless and testing all barriers. He eats grass and tortoise food but seems less interested in eating. We’ve had rain so I thought maybe he senses the monsoon and is seeking high ground? We give him a big water dish to go in and have a container we soak him in in the afternoons when it’s been 115. When he first came he was happy to eat for hours on grass, but he has gotten so restless he isn’t eating much. I’d like to give him other food but not sure what he is ok to eat other than the occasional strawberry. He lives outdoors at the desert botanical garden in his own little yard and we have him in our backyard. But it has been HOT! He has a burrow we made him that is shaded as well and frequently overnights outside of his burrow. He will go home to the garden after they are finished with the remodel of the house attached to his yard. But I just want to make sure he is as happy as possible.

text_content: A few years ago, a red footed tortoise just showed up in our south Jersey backyard, we found him eating cat food we leave out for a feral.  I thought he was some huge mutant box turtle - who would think an exotic species would just wander into your yard?  Either someone dumped him, or he escaped from an outside enclosure.  He ate all the cat food, and we put out some more, which he also ate, then he spent the night in our flower bed.  The next day I showed a picture of him to my brother, who identified him as a red foot, and I rushed home, hoping he was still snoozing in the garden, and thank goodness he was.  I called a few turtle people in the area, but got the impression from some that if they took him, he might then get sold. I contacted Garden State Tortoise and Chris agreed to give him a home.  My niece and I took the 40 minute drive to drop him off and asked Chris if we could see a little of the place when we got there.  I don’t think it is open to the public, but Chris was so nice and so gracious and showed us around.  It was such a relief to know the tortoise would be living in what is pretty much tortoise heaven, and that he would have other tortoises of his species around, and someone knowledgeable, and responsible, to look out for him.  I hope he is doing well.  Thanks so much, Chris, for all you do!

text_content: I've had a tortoise rob in my family for 50 years now. My grandpa found him as a baby with a yolk Sac near the mountains when he was 15. He roams in my grandpa's backyard and gets to live in nature. Remember if you keep him enclosed the tortoises life span will naturally decrease. In nature they can live for way longer. He enjoys the sunlight , drinks water from ponds and after a long day in the burrow he might sit with my grandpa at the patio. He eats once a day at best because they somehow seem to conserve their energy resources very efficiently. He's really quiet and has a great memory. We have a huge lettuce farm behind the yard so that's what he has been eating with occasional watermelon treats

text_content: I have had tortoises and box turtles for eight years. They are a ton of work to get set up properly but after that they are pretty low maintenance actually. But they have more personality than you would think they do. They also require more space than you think they would. For the right person and situation they make a great pet. Being able to keep them outside for part of the year is a great thing, I’m glad I can do that. And I think using a stock tank is a great idea for turtles or tortoises in certain situations. They are cheap and easy to set up.

One of the great things about living in the Deep South is around 7-8 months out of the year you can keep tortoises outside. One of the best parts about Sulcata’s is that their salads aren’t really that bad because they can literally eat certain common grasses on top of relatively cheap veggies. 
We have a year old female sulcata and we’ve been blown away by how much character and personality they have. Joined some FB care groups and people post videos of them calling the tortoises almost like a dog and it’ll come to them. 

The main thing about the diet especially if you’re giving large amounts of grass or even a little is always make sure the grass isn’t being treated either with toxic fertilizer or herbicides

I have had him for a couple of years now and he seems to be doing great!  Thank you for your videos and for all of your great information! text_content:
Had a Russian tortoise as a kid and she was a very sweet animal. She lived in an indoor pen at night and during the colder months, but during the day in the warmer months she got to live in an outdoor pen, with all of her favorite greens and veggies growing all over it. She knew it was feeding time when you tapped on her plate, and would come out of her burrow to eat. She loved being handled and would climb on us. 

Unfortunaty, she was a pet store tortoise and wasn't fron a reputable breeder, so she only made it to about 10 years old before she died of some hereditary disease. We were devastated when she had to go.

text_content:
As someone invested in learning about reptile cognition I appreciate you explaining why animals do behaviors people tend to think are stupid. Like how understanding transparent barriers is not something nature necessarily brings up. 

My favorite bit of scientists being weirdly human centric in evaluating the intelligence of a reptile is some psychologists deciding bearded dragons are smarter than tortoises because bearded dragons fall for the same optical illusions that humans do. Because intelligence is judged on such a human centric scale that being stupid in the same way as humans is considered a sign of intelligence.

On that same paper, I learned that scientists have determined that mango jelly is the treat tortoises love most. Or at least it's the treat they're most willing to participate in a scientific experiment in order to get. So if you ever need to bribe a tortoise that might be useful. This is based on cherryheads but a friend of mine who has sulcatas says it's also true for them.

text_content: I recently rescued a russian tortoise. Though not "abused", I'm afraid he was quite neglected. He was being kept in a large, plastic tub, under the dining room table without UVA/UVB lights, and without any special source of heat, other than what ever temp the mobile home was kept at, in Colorado. Not sure if he's ever really been outside, but I know his substrate was wood chips from their front yard (not good). I'm guessing his diet hasn't been the best either, but at least his waste was removed often. I believe they loved him, but didn't have any idea how to care for him. He is a trooper, though. Despite all this, he appears to be ok. I was in no way prepared to take him in, but did so gladly. I'd love if you could do an episode about shell rot and recognizing deficiencies in tortoises, and how to help them. My little guy has serious issues with his shell and I don't know what to do. Thanks!

text_content:
For any one living in the UK watching this, our equivalent to collard greens is called spring greens. Took me weeks of searching supermarkets, small independent fruit and veg shops, market stalls and trying to find other tortoise owners to ask before I found it. Collard greens don't grow here, but spring greens are basically identical and can be used as a staple part of their diet - Morrison's do a big bag of the stuff for about £1. Also endives are usually called chicory over here.

Just want to add as well that one of the best things you can feed them is just generic garden weeds like dandelion (mostly the leaves, the flower is good for them but only about one a week) - just make sure that they don't have any pesticides or weed-killer on them.

text_content: I love my russian Walter! He is the craziest little tortoise ever! With Russians you can actually use the vertical space since they LOVE to climb! I have a second floor in the indoor enclosure and he loves it. I also found very easy to manage the humidity by lining the bottom with plastic tarp and actually planting the furniture plants in the substrate, they need to be watered often so they help maintain the humidity in the ground where they want to borrow (although don’t get too attached to the plants he/she will must likely try to climb it) We also have a huge yard where he goes a few times a week and he exercises going all over the place several times, this gives him the exercise he needs and direct sunlight. Mazuri pellets for the diet with fresh greenies, the flowers of the season and a calcium block at all times. Change the greenies often and include some red, orange, or yellow veggies like squash, pumpkin, carrots but give just a few times a month, this gives them important nutrients missing in veggies of other colors.

text_content: Oh yes. Shelly made it perfectly clear he did not want his enclosure anymore. He’d spend hours banging on that glass, climbing up the texture background. He was keeping me up at night. Now he free roams the first floor. He has a good half of the living room with blankets, hamster hay nets, and logs to dig and climb. I’ve set up his lamps over a dog bed with 2 towels underneath and 2 blankets on top. He arranges his stuff himself to make hiding caves or climbing areas. He turned a plastic tub lid into a slide. He always has a mixed greens salad with water and a plate of tortoise pellets with water that I’ve heated up in the microwave for a minute. I call it his oatmeal. It’s his favorite. Sometimes that’s all he wants is warm rehydrated tortoise pellets. The cat likes to steal his kale and use it as a toy. Shelly likes to watch her throw it around the room from his bed. The cat treats him like he’s “rock baby”. She follows him around and announces when he’s doing things she doesn’t like. I also sleep on the couch in the living room so I can monitor the pets. I’ve woken up to find he’s climbed onto the couch and he’s shoved the front half of himself under my pillow with the cat curled up behind him. It’s really weird.

text_content:
I recommend letting your tortoise roam around your house for a few hours a day, so long as it's a safe environment. Even a good sized enclosure isn't big enough to keep them in there 24/7, so I often let mine roam inside the house or in my yard. 

I would also recommend socializing your tortoise and spending a lot of time with them. 

Lastly and most importantly, give your tortoise lots of love! Be kind and gentle with how you speak to and handle them. Pet them everyday and develop a routine to which they can look forward.

text_content: I have box turtles and a Russian tortoise. I put my Russian tortoise in a 150 gallon Rubbermaid cattle tank outside during the summer. My box turtles have the run of my entire backyard because my fence is buried 2 feet underground and set in a concrete footer. I don't let my Russian tortoise and box turtles near each other because of possible fighting or disease transmission. My Russian tortoise is a female. I've been into keeping and breeding reptiles for 39 years now and also have snakes and leopard geckos. Except for the box turtles I have gotten away from the breeding aspect of keeping. There's not much I can do about the box turtles because they all live outside together. Right now my box turtles are hibernating and I won't see them until sometime in April.

I have 2 Horsefields and one of them is an absolute pain to keep hydrated. She absolutely HATES water, I keep them separately because the other is a male and he's extremely aggressive, but I literally can't even give her a water bowl because minutes after I place it in she poops and pees in it every single time, so I soak her twice a week to make up for that but she will not drink, she just kind of raises up as high as she can to get away from the water and if I'm lucky she goes 5 minutes before pooping. I occasionally give her something like watermelon to try and get some hydration in her and she absolutely loves that... but she loves it too much and is incredibly stubborn so then refuses to eat pretty much anything else for up to a week afterwards. It sucks because she starts to get slightly sunken in eyes every now and again and I know that's because she's dehydrated, I've had her checked out and she's 100% healthy otherwise, she's growing well, she's a healthy weight and her shell is absolutely perfect, but she just WILL NOT drink.

Sorry for the rant, just been researching like crazy trying to find some kind of solution and I can't seem to find anything that's helped, even tried using a syringe to get water into her mouth but she's very shy and annoyingly smart so the second she sees the damn thing she locks herself into her shell so there's no way I'm getting it into her mouth. Just at a loss.

text_content: One of my tortoises is obsessed with the tortoise diet pellets and will often refuse to eat anything else for nearly a week after having them. I know it's not the worst thing for them to be addicted to as they are good for them, but I only really have them as a backup for when I can't get a good variety of food for them (I don't have a garden so I can't always get hold of the natural foods they eat, especially since the pandemic started) and it's really hard to get her to eat anything else just from giving her them once in a while to make sure she's definitely getting the nutrients she needs. They can be very stubborn when it comes to food.

text_content:
I have had my tortoise for 29 years now - He is 44 years old😊 At first there wasn’t internet and in the books it was not always correct information.

Could you tell me about your substrate, because I’ve tried a lot of things and there comes bugs and different kind of animals in it even if I clean of it of pee and poo. What do you do, to keep these away? And underneath it what have you put there?

About bath, I only do it once in a while, if I can see he can’t push his poo out, or if he has a lot of pee on him. As you said it’s not normal for them in the wild. I put him in the bath room sink he loves water smoothly running over him, he loves that and then afterwards little water he can sit in. They must only be in there max 15-20 min. because otherwise they can get worm I have read. I only tooth brush his behind quickly, and if you notice your tortoise doesn’t seem to like the tooth brush, and mine doesn’t either, so I only do it shortly on his behind to remove poo etc. on his behind. 

About food it has been hard for me to get him to eat other things, because at first I didn’t know about all of these things and he got a lot he shouldn’t have. Earlier on I went about two weeks, (they can live without food about three weeks), where he didn’t eat the other things, and he “won” but now I put it on top on his food and hope he gets more used to it, but he is sneaky and try his best to avoid it, if it’s possible😊

When it comes to fruits occasional bits of apple is okay.
It’s actually one of the few fruits that’s not too bad to give them.
Fruits like strawberries are high in (a certain mineral I forget) but anyway if I remember correctly that can lead to things one of which is pyramiding.
Fruit isn’t completely bad for them though.
Treat fruits like they’re an extremely over the top sugary treat that tastes amazing but is something you really shouldn’t have.

All I’m saying is be very sparing with fruits and always look up whether a certain food is harmful to your tortoise.
Actually that goes for ALL animals not just tortoises. Just because you can eat it does not mean your pet can.

video_title: RUSSIAN TORTOISES ARE THE BEST BEGINNER PET REPTILE!
video_description: Guys! I did it! I found the best beginner reptile! Let me explain my reasoning as to why a Russian Tortoise is the best for beginners ...
text_content:
Interesting choice! Of all the animals I have, my Greek Tortoise (similar to Russians in care) is the highest to maintain, almost as much as my cats. He has an outdoor enclosure for warm months and an indoor setup for cold months, he needs fresh weeds which I have to grow myself on top of supplemental fresh greens from the market, and his substrate needs a maintained humidity for proper shell growth. A BIG caveat unmentioned is the difference between caring for an adult tortoise VS baby tortoise, baby tortoises need to be absolutely dialed in or they will grow up with lots of health issues and are a bad long (VERY long) term investment if you're not on top of care, even hardy species. I would say turtles are some of the most popular reptiles but also least cared for correctly.
My snakes are hands down the easiest reptiles to feed and enclose. You brought up that tortoises 'teach' you about UVB, but I would say that giving a keeper more responsibilities does not make it easier to begin with. You can experiment with low UVB with your snake or leopard gecko, but you can't play around with tortoise UVB!

video_title: RUSSIAN TORTOISES ARE THE BEST BEGINNER PET REPTILE!
video_description: Guys! I did it! I found the best beginner reptile! Let me explain my reasoning as to why a Russian Tortoise is the best for beginners ...
text_content:
Wholeheartedly agree on your point about having to be passionate about keeping any reptile. A dog is easy, you just have to provide them with one of thousands of good food products that are readily available in any supermarket, a place to sleep, a daily walk and to train them in basic things like not going to the toilet indoors - you have to be particularly useless to be unable to meet their basic needs. Horsefield/Russian Tortoises don't require the same amount of attention as a dog and are far cheaper to care for, but they're still far more complex in terms of their basic needs and a lot of people don't do their research before buying them.

The unfortunate thing with their popularity as a "beginner reptile" and how easily available they are in comparison with other reptiles is that a lot of the time parents get them for their kids as a first pet thinking they're on a similar level to a hamster or guinea pig in terms of care, they don't realise how much work actually goes into the husbandry and often the kids get bored of them within a year and want a dog so you end up finding hundreds of poorly looked after tortoises being sold online that have health issues like MBD from lack of UVB, poor diet and being kept on improper substrate and they commonly perish at a far too young age from more serious problems like respiratory issues or even just stress from not having their basic needs met. The shop were I got my first one from didn't even give any information on how to care for them or ask if we had the right set up, I just walked in, pointed at the one I wanted, paid and walked out of the shop with it - luckily I'd already done my research, but I do worry about how many people do just walk in there and buy one on a whim without knowing a single thing about them. Whenever I've spoken to someone who's thinking about getting a tortoise I always recommend that they get one from a dedicated reptile shop or a trusted breeder who won't sell one to you unless you can prove you have the right set up and will provide you with a care sheet.

text_content: If you're looking for a cheap outside enclosure when he gets a little bigger. I bought a 19ft longx10ft wide and 6.5ft high chicken enclosure on Amazon for 220$. I'm expanding my 13ftx10ft one I already have for my male Russian tortoise. I adopted him from someone keeping him in a tiny tank so l wanted to spoil him. I put a lot of edible plants and weeds in using the tortoise table app. Added some hides, an underground tunnel, some hills, and a hamster wheel he surprising loves. Watching him enjoy it is beyond worth it and it's really not as much work as you'd think. I started with the basics, making sure the enclosure was escape and predator proof. Added a few plants and a hide. Then just kept adding from there. I also let my bearded dragon outside in it during the day and leopard gecko for a few hours at night.

text_content: I have one he's about 5 years old and about the size of a full grown desert tortoise. He's strong as hell as well. He's also highly intelligent for what he is. Makes a good pet but you've got to have the right environment. If you live somewhere cold don't bother unless you're rich and can afford to keep him in a warm garage with uv bulbs and heat lamps all around along with maintaining his diet. They do not brumate (almost like hibernation but not exactly) in the winter like most other tortoises. Warm places are perfect for them. They can dig and they will make a long tunnel be prepared for that. Outside of that they're cool as hell and definitely will outlive you.

text_content: Clint, I appreciate this,  I have read and watched anything I can on these as it's my favorite  turtle! I legitimately  was just saying there isn't a lot of information on the mata mata, and I wake up to this. I  rescued a baby mata mata from someone local here who kept him in a plastic container with no filter barely fed. I have had him two years now in a pond setup, I do use a sand, media sump and uv filter.  The room stays very humid as the frogs and geckos I keep require it and I keep it temp regulated, my ph stays around 6.0 to 6.5 he feeds on different live feeders, I do use a supplement with as well. I will say I loved this video because it gave even more insight to a turtle that is truly hard to keep as I do upkeep daily I test the water daily also. Truly thank you for this video and giving a  great insight to how hard they are to keep

text_content: I have a mata mata del Rio orinoco taken at a turtle fair in 2019.Now for two years she has been living in a preformed pond that I installed internally at home. I alternate frozen fish with live fish. I also tried other types of food that were never considered by her. Acidify water from time to time by adding catappa leaf. The temperature of the winter water. It is around the 24' in the hottest spot. Out of the water I only have two neon LEDs for the plants. Being that the water evaporates, the right moist condensation is created for when it comes out to breathe and I refill hot water weekly. So far I haven't had any health problems and it has reached a fair size. Now that she is grown I feed her twice a week, but sometimes I leave fish in the pond that last several times/days and that she loves to 😁😁 hunt. As a child, however, she ate almost every day. But even as a child there was no way to make them eat worms, insects or other food, she preferred to starve rather even though on some groups they said she could eat anything other than fish. At the moment I have never included heated outdoor lamps even though the room is not particularly warm in winter. I use a pond filter pump with a uvc lamp and the inserted plants also help to keep the water clean.

text_content: Hey clint! I dont keep mata mata, but i do keep a little blackwater tank, and some of the reading ive done suggests that bacteria may be the most important aspect of keeping animals adapted to these environments. The pH of these environments is often too low for benefitial bacteria important to the nitrogen cycle and for harmful bacteria which results in lowered immunities especially for wild caught animals. This means that if you give them a naturalistic environment you increase the risk of poor water quality issues and if you dont you risk exposing them to bacteria theyre not adapted to surviving. UV treating the water is a really smart choice if this is relevant to mata mata like it is to other orinoco river animals.

Matamata Turtles are lone-wolf types. They don’t like to mix with other creatures, so they shouldn’t be handled often. You don’t find them moving around that much either, which means that they aren’t the most energetic viewing companion.
Although they aren’t cuddly or zestful, they are very easy to care for. If you wanted a low-maintenance pet, the Matamata would be a great friend. Their unique appearance also means that your lethargic buddy will still be very interesting to watch.

I'm Brazilian and if this turtle genuinely came from the Amazon forest and was not raised in captivity it's possible that it was illegally trafficked. In Brazil, the sale and possession of Mata-Mata turtles are illegal according to the Environmental Crimes Law (Law No. 9,605/1998), and the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA) also regulates the possession and trade of native species. I highly recommend you guys to research where this fish store sourced this turtle!!!

The sale and trade of Mata-Mata turtles for abroad can be legal in some cases, as long as all applicable laws and regulations are followed, both in the country of origin and the destination country. To legally export a Mata-Mata turtle as a pet, the exporter must obtain a CITES export permit from the country of origin and ensure that all local laws related to the possession and trade of wildlife are respected. In addition, the importer must ensure that the trade and possession of the turtle are legal in the destination country and, if necessary, obtain a CITES import permit or other relevant authorizations. You should ask the pet store for documentation regarding the origin of the Mata-Mata turtle and any required permits, such as CITES import permits or any other relevant authorizations.

text_content:
Nick I would get rid of the fake plant but add a lot of leaf litter, Indian Almond leaves, Oak leaves and the like. They need lots of tannen in the water and the leaves will help with that as well as give it the proper environment to trully feel safe and comfortable.
As the tank is I would only give it a 4 max 5. Also for the next time you want to add driftwood to a tank get a trashcan, completely clean and disinfected, and soak if long before you add it. It should already be waterlogged and not floating before you add it to a tank.

Dear Brian,

text_content:
Lovely wee guy! Thank you for working so well on him. He looks much happier now!!

I'm always amazed at captured turtles that do well in captivity from thw wild. About 25ish years ago, an ex and I came upon 2 baby red-eared sliders in 2 different spots about 6 weeks apart when fishing. One had just lost an eye- it was a raw mess, and one just lost a leg. We got them home and I had an old tank that was empty so to the petstore we went for supplies, got them seen at a vet, and got their wounds cleaned. They were healing and doing ok, eating and drinking and pooping. However around 2 weeks in, both began flipping out. The one missing an eye would smack himself into the same corner repeatedly. The other one began something similar but not as violent. I have no idea why, the vet also had no idea why but just said that although we gave them a large tank with water and good substrate, that they still were likely unhappy being confined and recommended us to let them go. We brought them both to a quieter pond that didn't have large fish or snapping turtles and let them go. I always wondered 1. if they survived to adulthood with one minus an eye and one minus a limb and 2. If so, would they find each other and live together somehow. I never set out to bring home baby turtles, I couldn't just leave them like that. I hope they made it.

Its sounds like you talked to the teacher about changing the diet and cage conditions. I do hope they listen to you. It was nice of the parent to offer to do this, but how many more years before another parent also offers. I dunno exactly HOW much pro bono work you do. But maybe this is also an opportunity to educate others? do you maybe plan to keep up with the turtle? Maybe you could go in once a year as like a "guest" and part of the class that day might be you grooming the turtle in front of the children while educating them a little bit about birds and reptiles? prolly not worth it, you having such a busy schedule and all, unless the school was willing to pay your grooming fee. lol. Still, the teacher might be up for that once a year. 

I had someone bring in a butterfly chrysalis to my first grade class room and educate us about butterflies once, and then they left us the chrysalis so we could all watch it hatch, come time. It is one of my clearest early school memories. You would be surprised how effective animals can be as teaching aides! :)

text_content: I raised a batch of betta fry and yes you have to cull the disfigured ones. But i put one in its own tank. It lived and grew, but remained deformed and not as large as his brothers. Because he could not fit his organs properly as they continued to grow but but his spine was not, he eventually passed at a year old. He lived a good life with live plants and quality food and was always happy to swim when you came near. Unfortunately, animals with deformities may not live as long, although they are given the best care possible.

text_content:
Whether humans or animals, when they’re sick and dying, there’s always that “last hurrah” that makes it look like they’re improving, just right before they pass. It always gives you false hope. I’ve experienced it one too many times. 

Really sorry for your loss. Thanks for giving them everything you could and opening a new world to all of us. 🤍

May their legacy live on with the rest of your two-headed pets. ❤️‍🩹

video_title: RIP Barf & Belch
text_content: I follow a lot of hospice workers, & they talk about terminal patients getting bursts of energy & lucidity that make the family think they're recovering right before the end. I think the same thing happened with our parakeet right before she passed, & it's probably also what happened with Barf & Belch. Sorry for your loss, at least they got to feel better for a bit before they left… Rest in peace, little ones. 💐

text_content: Extra energy/liveliness is normal at the end of life for many creatures, including humans. They're probably very grateful in whatever kind of turtle afterlife there is. Don't get down on yourself, if they weren't under your care, they probably wouldn't have made it at all, much less over a year.

text_content: When I was a kid, my sibling and I found a baby yellow-bellied slider at a local lake. We raised that turtle for at least 4 years before realizing we were unable to keep up with his maintenance. We used to take him out of his tank and let him wander around the house (but we had cats, so he would get covered in cat fur :/) My dad ended up contacting a local reptile rescue who agreed to take him in and I'm so grateful they did. Looking back, I know we didn't care for him right even though we tried our best. But he's likely happy and healthy now with a big pond and proper food. I greatly appreciate seeing you, and other reptile rescues,  take in wonderful guys like these <3

text_content: I work as a park naturalist and I spend a lot of time informing visitors what turtle care is actually like. Our turtle ambassadors are very charming and sweet, but half my full-time job is taking care of them. I always tell interested families that turtles can survive a great range of conditions, but thrive in a very narrow range. Is your child ready to ensure that the turtle's heat, UVB, humidity, space, omnivorous diet, water quality, and tank clean needs are met every day? The answer is almost certainly no. There may be beginner reptiles, but no reptile is a beginner pet. I hope I get through to some people.

text_content: It sounds like Biggins is in gut stasis/bloat. I have guinea pigs and I know they're not turtles / tortoises. But with my guinea pigs, when the gut doesn't move it's called GI stasis / bloat. Apparently Biggins gut isn't moving. I saw that what you're going to feed him is Emeraid critical care. I've had to use that with my guinea pigs multiple times. It works well!! I'm so glad to hear that Biggins doesn't have to have surgery. That has to be a relief to you all. I've grown very fond of Biggins. I do hope and pray that the next video we see from GST is a video of Biggins pooping. The times that my guinea pigs went into gut stasis I've told my husband many  times, I can't believe how excited I am to see poop!!

text_content:
I do! The ficus is often called banana leaf ficus.  Also known as ‘Maclellandii’. I know this tree very well because it’s my abosolute favorite ficus. It’s more beautiful than the f. Benjamina or weeping fig and it doesn’t drop leaves nearly as fast as the others, should you forget to water it or when you move it to different conditions. That’s as a potted tree. You can root it from branch cuttings, but it takes awhile to form that trunk. But it’s a beautiful bush too, if you trim the branches and make it multi-stem. It’s best for DEEP shade, to part shade. But never full sun in Florida. It gets pale, burned, yellow leaves in full sun. I like in morning sun- afternoon shade.
I’ve never seen the fruit before! Great plant!❤

Don't let the haters get in your head. Anybody that keeps numerous animals ones in their lifetime experiences death due to circumstances like disease or environmental conditions. And if haters say that they did not than they are not keeping animals long enough or they eventually will experience the same matter.

I experienced the same thing when I moved and the heat in the reptile room died. Several animals died and some became sick and died also due to sickness from the cold. That doesn't mean that I take bad care off my animals, it is just bad luck.

You take care of your animals very well. Make sure you learn from this long cold spell and take appropriate measures sonit doesn't happen again.

video_title: Dog’s Camera Snaps Whenever He’s Excited #furparents #dog #animalphotography
video_description: Nikon Asia made a very interesting project called Heartography. They put a camera on a dog that takes pictures whenever the dog ...
text_content: This innovative project, Heartography, was developed by Nikon Asia. They attached a camera to a dog that automatically takes photos whenever his heart rate spikes, capturing what excites him most. The system uses a heart rate monitor connected to a microcontroller inside the camera case, which triggers the shutter when the dog’s heartbeat rises. This way, we get a unique view of a dog’s perspective, from other animals and food to everyday objects that spark curiosity.
